1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

6	import Mathlib

8	import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.WeilDivisor
9	
10	/-!
11	# The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point and its global sections (RR.3)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.3** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the
28	  Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`, the `H¹`-vanishing
29	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a genus-`0` curve, the dimension formula
30	  `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`, and the existence of a non-constant
31	  rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with at most a simple pole at `P`.
32	- `RR.4` (`RationalCurveIso.lean`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34	
35	## Status (iter-183 Lane A — sig amend + carrierSet scaffold)
36	
37	Iter-183 Lane A (re-dispatch from iter-182 deferral) landed:
38	
39	1. **Sig amend** `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` and `toFunctionField` now take
40	   the codimension-one witness `(hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)` explicitly,
41	   so the subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier can read off the order at `P` via the
42	   prime divisor `⟨P, hPcoh⟩`. The amend matches the blueprint chapter prose
43	   for the Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` direct construction (per analogist
44	   `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md`, Decision 3 + Decision 4 verdict
45	   `ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
46	2. **Scaffold** `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet` (concrete `Set`-valued
47	   substantive carrier — the set of rational functions with the order
48	   conditions on a given open). This is iter-183's substantive
49	   contribution beyond the sig amend; no new `sorry` introduced.
50	3. The bodies of `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` (L140) and `toFunctionField`
51	   (L154) remain typed `sorry` for iter-184+ (the full chain
52	   `carrierSet → carrierSubmodule (Submodule) → presheaf (Functor) →
53	   isSheaf (typed sorry) → Sheaf` is ~230-360 LOC; iter-183's helper
54	   budget = 5 and `sorry` ceiling = 7 forced PARTIAL).
55	
56	The 5 pinned declarations are:
57	
58	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint` — the invertible
59	   sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` associated to a closed point `P` on a smooth proper
60	   curve `C / k̄`.
61	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff`
62	   — the identification of global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` with the
63	   Riemann–Roch space
64	   `L([P]) = {f ∈ K(C)^× | div(f) + [P] ≥ 0} ∪ {0}`,
65	   expressed as an `Iff`-style characterisation of the order conditions
66	   `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
67	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero`
68	   — the cohomological vanishing `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a smooth proper
69	   geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, via the long
70	   exact sequence of the closed-point short exact sequence
71	   `0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0`.
72	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`
73	   — the dimension formula `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2` in genus `0`,
74	   specialising the χ-identity `RR.2` to `D = [P]` and consuming the
75	   `H¹`-vanishing of pin 3.
76	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero`
77	   — the corollary: a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` regular
78	   on `C \ {P}` with at most a simple pole at `P`, obtained as a lift of
79	   any non-zero element of the quotient
80	   `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) / 𝓀̄ · 1`.
81	
82	## Notation reminders
83	
84	The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` is realised as a
85	`Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`,
86	the same `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured sheaf category used by the project's
87	`Scheme.HModule` cohomology pipeline (cf. `AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`).
88	Its `H⁰` and `H¹` are computed via `Scheme.HModule kbar (·) 0` and
89	`Scheme.HModule kbar (·) 1`, both of which carry a canonical `Module k̄`
90	instance.
91

98	with controlled pole), IV §1 pp. 294–297 (Riemann–Roch and the genus-`0`
99	specialisation, Example 1.3.5 and Exercise 1.1). Stacks Project tags
100	01X0 (line bundle of a Cartier divisor), 0BE5 (the global sections

110	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
111	
112	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
113	
114	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
115	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
116	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
117	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
118	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
119	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
120	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/
121	
122	namespace Scheme
123	
124	/-! ### Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier (iter-183 Lane A scaffold)
125	
126	The substantive iter-183 contribution: a concrete per-open `Set`-valued
127	carrier of the line bundle `𝒪_C(P)`, realised directly as Hartshorne's
128	subsheaf of the function-field constant sheaf `K_C` (Hartshorne II §6
129	p. 144; analogist `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md` Decision 3
130	`ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
131	
132	A section of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U` is a rational function `f ∈ K(C)`
133	satisfying the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
134	`Q ≠ P` with `Q.point ∈ U` (regularity on the complement of `P` inside
135	`U`) and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
136	The construction is independent of the choice of uniformiser at `P`:
137	any two uniformisers differ by a unit, so the order-`≥ −1` condition at
138	`P` is intrinsic.
139	
140	iter-184+ will upgrade `carrierSet` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the
141	closure proofs (zero / addition / kbar-scalar), bundle the result as a
142	presheaf functor (identity-on-`K(C)` restrictions, monotone in `U` via
143	`carrierSet_mono`), and discharge the sheaf property via gluing-by-
144	stalks (stalk-locality of the order conditions at each prime divisor). -/
145	
146	/-- **Carrier set** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U : (Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`: the
147	set of rational functions `f ∈ K(C)` satisfying the order conditions
148	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q` with `Q.point ∈ U.unop`,
149	`Q.point ≠ P`, and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U.unop`.
150	
151	iter-183 Lane A landed this as a concrete substantive `Set` definition;
152	no sorry. -/
153	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet
154	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
156	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
157	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
158	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
159	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
160	    Set C.left.functionField := by
161	  let Phat : C.left.PrimeDivisor := ⟨P, hPcoh⟩
162	  haveI := Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
163	    (X := C.left) Phat
164	  exact { f | (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ∈ U.unop.1 → Q.point ≠ P →
165	              0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
166	              (P ∈ U.unop.1 → (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Phat f) }
167	
168	/-- **Monotonicity of `carrierSet` in `U`**: when `V.unop ⊆ U.unop` (i.e.
169	the open `V` is contained in the open `U`), the carrier on `U` is
170	INCLUDED in the carrier on `V` (the order conditions over the smaller
171	open `V` involve fewer prime divisors, hence are easier to satisfy).
172	
173	This is the substantive monotonicity that drives the (identity-on-`K(C)`)
174	restriction map of the would-be `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf` functor:
175	in `(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`, an arrow `U ⟶ V` corresponds to `V.unop ⊆ U.unop`,
176	and the restriction map `carrierSet U → carrierSet V` is the inclusion
177	delivered by this lemma.
178	
179	iter-183 Lane A landed this as the substantive monotonicity proof; no
180	sorry. -/
181	private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
183	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
184	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
185	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
186	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
187	    {U V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ}
188	    (hUV : V.unop.1 ⊆ U.unop.1) :
189	    lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
190	      ⊆ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh V := by
191	  intro f hf
192	  refine ⟨fun Q hQV hQP => hf.1 Q (hUV hQV) hQP, fun hPV => hf.2 (hUV hPV)⟩
193	
194	/-- **Nonempty top-open**: for an integral scheme `X`, the top open is
195	nonempty (its carrier is `Set.univ` and `X` itself is nonempty since
196	`IsIntegral X ⟹ IrreducibleSpace X ⟹ Nonempty X`). Supplied as an
197	instance so that the standard Mathlib `Algebra Γ(X, U) X.functionField`
198	instance fires at `U = ⊤`. -/
199	instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instNonemptyTopOpen
200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] :
201	    Nonempty ((⊤ : X.Opens) : Scheme) :=
202	  (Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff (U := (⊤ : X.Opens))).mpr
203	    ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty X).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
204	
205	/-- **`kbar`-algebra structure on the function field `K(C)`**. The
206	project's `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.algebraSection` instance gives
207	`Algebra kbar Γ(C.left, ⊤)`, and Mathlib's standard
208	`AlgebraicGeometry.instAlgebraCarrierObjOppositeOpens...` gives
209	`Algebra Γ(C.left, ⊤) K(C)` via `germToFunctionField`. Composing the
210	two algebra maps via `RingHom.toAlgebra` produces the desired
211	`Algebra kbar K(C)` instance, which is needed for `Submodule kbar K(C)`
212	to type-check. -/
213	noncomputable instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instAlgebraKbarFunctionField
214	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
215	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsIntegral C.left] :
216	    Algebra kbar C.left.functionField := by
217	  haveI : Nonempty (⊤ : C.left.Opens) :=
218	    (AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff
219	      (X := C.left) (U := (⊤ : C.left.Opens))).mpr
220	      ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty C.left).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
221	  exact RingHom.toAlgebra
222	    ((Scheme.germToFunctionField C.left (⊤ : C.left.Opens)).hom.comp
223	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C
224	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))).hom)
225	
226	/-- **Carrier submodule** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U`: upgrade of
227	`carrierSet U` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the three closure proofs
228	(`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`). The closure proofs rest on the DVR-shipped
229	`Ring.ordFrac_add` (non-archimedean inequality on the discrete valuation
230	of a regular-in-codim-1 stalk; iter-186 Step 1 upgraded the carrier class
231	so this is invocable) and `Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit` (the scalar from
232	`kbar` becomes a unit in the stalk, preserving the order under scalar
233	multiplication).
234	
235	iter-186 Step 2 (per analogist `ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
236	Decision 2): structural skeleton with the load-bearing Mathlib lemmas in
237	place; bookkeeping `sorry`s remain for the prover phase. -/
238	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule
239	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
240	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
241	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
242	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
243	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
244	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
245	    Submodule kbar C.left.functionField where
246	  carrier := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
247	  zero_mem' := by
248	    -- `order Y 0 = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0`;
249	    -- both `0 ≤ 0` and `-1 ≤ 0` hold trivially.
250	    refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩
251	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
252	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
253	  add_mem' := by
254	    -- Case-split on `f + g = 0` (trivial via `WithZero.log_zero`), else
255	    -- apply `Ring.ordFrac_add` (DVR-shipped, Step 1 unlocks) and
256	    -- `WithZero.log` monotonicity on the nonzero part.
257	    rintro a b ⟨ha₁, ha₂⟩ ⟨hb₁, hb₂⟩
258	    have hMNZ : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (x : C.left.functionField),
259	        x ≠ 0 → Ring.ordFrac (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point) x ≠ 0 := by
260	      intro Q x hx; simp [hx]
261	    have key : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ),
262	        n ≤ 0 → n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q a →
263	          n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q b →
264	          n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (a + b) := by
265	      intro Q n hn haₚ hbₚ
266	      by_cases hab : a + b = 0
267	      · simpa [hab, Scheme.RationalMap.order] using hn
268	      by_cases hae : a = 0
269	      · rw [hae, zero_add]; exact hbₚ
270	      by_cases hbe : b = 0
271	      · rw [hbe, add_zero]; exact haₚ
272	      set R := C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point
273	      have hoa : Ring.ordFrac R a ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q a hae
274	      have hob : Ring.ordFrac R b ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q b hbe
275	      have hoab : Ring.ordFrac R (a + b) ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q _ hab
276	      have hmin : Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b ≤ Ring.ordFrac R (a + b) :=
277	        Ring.ordFrac_add (R := R) a b hab
278	      have hlog : (Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b).log ≤
279	          Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (a + b) := by
280	        rcases min_cases (Ring.ordFrac R a) (Ring.ordFrac R b) with
281	            ⟨heq, _⟩ | ⟨heq, _⟩
282	        · rw [heq]; exact (WithZero.log_le_log hoa hoab).mpr (heq ▸ hmin)
283	        · rw [heq]; exact (WithZero.log_le_log hob hoab).mpr (heq ▸ hmin)
284	      have hminbd : n ≤ (Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b).log := by
285	        rcases min_cases (Ring.ordFrac R a) (Ring.ordFrac R b) with
286	            ⟨heq, _⟩ | ⟨heq, _⟩
287	        · rw [heq]; exact haₚ
288	        · rw [heq]; exact hbₚ
289	      linarith
290	    refine ⟨fun Q hQU hQP => key Q 0 le_rfl (ha₁ Q hQU hQP) (hb₁ Q hQU hQP),
291	      fun hPU => key ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ (-1) (by norm_num) (ha₂ hPU) (hb₂ hPU)⟩
292	  smul_mem' := by
293	    -- For `c : kbar`, the scalar action `c • f` on `K(C)` factors through
294	    -- `algebraMap kbar K(C)`. For `c ≠ 0`, `algebraMap kbar K(C) c` lifts to
295	    -- a nonzero `β` in every stalk via the germ map; `Ring.ordFrac_ge_one_of_ne_zero`
296	    -- on `β` then gives `order Q (algebraMap c) ≥ 0`, hence `c•x ∈ carrierSet`.
297	    intro c x ⟨hx₁, hx₂⟩
298	    rcases eq_or_ne c 0 with rfl | hc
299	    · -- `c = 0` ⇒ `0 • x = 0` ∈ carrierSet via `zero_mem'`.
300	      simp only [zero_smul]
301	      refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩ <;> simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
302	    rcases eq_or_ne x 0 with rfl | hx_ne
303	    · -- `x = 0` ⇒ `c • 0 = 0` ∈ carrierSet.
304	      simp only [smul_zero]
305	      refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩ <;> simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
306	    -- Both `c ≠ 0` and `x ≠ 0`: compute `order Q (c•x)` via multiplicativity.
307	    have hsmul : c • x = (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) * x :=
308	      Algebra.smul_def c x
309	    have hαne : (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) ≠ 0 := by
310	      have hinj := FaithfulSMul.algebraMap_injective kbar C.left.functionField
311	      simpa using hinj.ne_iff.mpr hc
312	    have hMNZ : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (y : C.left.functionField),
313	        y ≠ 0 → Ring.ordFrac (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point) y ≠ 0 := by
314	      intro Q y hy; simp [hy]
315	    have key_alpha_ge : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor),
316	        0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q
317	              (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) := by
318	      intro Q
319	      set R := C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point
320	      let β : R := (C.left.presheaf.germ (⊤ : C.left.Opens) Q.point trivial).hom
321	        ((Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C
322	            (Opposite.op (⊤ : C.left.Opens))).hom c)
323	      have hα_eq : (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) =
324	          algebraMap R C.left.functionField β := by
325	        change ((Scheme.germToFunctionField C.left (⊤ : C.left.Opens)).hom
326	            ((Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C
327	                (Opposite.op (⊤ : C.left.Opens))).hom c)) =
328	            (C.left.presheaf.stalkSpecializes
329	              ((genericPoint_spec C.left).specializes trivial)).hom β
330	        rw [← TopCat.Presheaf.germ_stalkSpecializes_apply
331	          (h := (genericPoint_spec C.left).specializes trivial)]
332	      have hβne : β ≠ 0 := by
333	        intro hzero; apply hαne; rw [hα_eq, hzero, map_zero]
334	      have hαord_ne : Ring.ordFrac R
335	          (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q _ hαne
336	      have hge : (1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)) ≤
337	          Ring.ordFrac R (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) := by
338	        rw [hα_eq]; exact Ring.ordFrac_ge_one_of_ne_zero hβne
339	      unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
340	      rw [show (0 : ℤ) = WithZero.log (1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)) by simp]
341	      exact (WithZero.log_le_log (by norm_num) hαord_ne).mpr hge
342	    have key : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ),
343	        n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q x →
344	        n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (c • x) := by
345	      intro Q n hxn
346	      rw [hsmul]
347	      set R := C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point
348	      have hαnez := hMNZ Q _ hαne
349	      have hxnez := hMNZ Q x hx_ne
350	      unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
351	      rw [map_mul, WithZero.log_mul hαnez hxnez]
352	      have := key_alpha_ge Q
353	      unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order at this hxn
354	      linarith
355	    refine ⟨fun Q hQU hQP => key Q 0 (hx₁ Q hQU hQP),
356	      fun hPU => key ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ (-1) (hx₂ hPU)⟩
357	
358	/-- **The carrier presheaf of `𝒪_C(P)`** (iter-187 Step 3 of the
359	`carrierSet → carrierSubmodule → carrierPresheaf → isSheaf → Sheaf`
360	recipe from `analogies/ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md` Decision 3).
361	
362	Bundles `carrierSubmodule P hPcoh U` as the per-open value of a functor
363	`(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ ⥤ ModuleCat kbar`. The restriction map for
364	`f : U ⟶ V` in `(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ` (i.e. `V.unop ⊆ U.unop` in opens)
365	is the `Submodule.inclusion` delivered by `carrierSet_mono`: as
366	`Submodule kbar K(C)`s, the carrier on `U` is included in the carrier
367	on `V`, and the inclusion is the identity-on-`K(C)` restriction. The
368	functor laws (`map_id`, `map_comp`) hold definitionally after
369	`Subtype`-extensionality. -/
370	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf
371	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
372	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
373	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
374	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
375	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
376	    (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ ⥤ ModuleCat.{u} kbar where
377	  obj U := ModuleCat.of kbar
378	    ↥(lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule P hPcoh U)
379	  map {U V} f := ModuleCat.ofHom <|
380	    Submodule.inclusion
381	      (p := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule P hPcoh U)
382	      (p' := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule P hPcoh V)
383	      (lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono P hPcoh
384	        (CategoryTheory.leOfHom f.unop))
385	  map_id U := by ext ⟨x, _⟩; rfl
386	  map_comp _ _ := by ext ⟨x, _⟩; rfl
387	
388	/-- **Sheaf property of `carrierPresheaf`** (iter-187 Step 4 of the
389	`carrierSet → … → Sheaf` recipe). The Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C`
390	carrier inherits the sheaf condition from the underlying `Type`-valued
391	sheaf condition on its forget: each `carrierSubmodule U` is, as a set,
392	a subset of the function field `K(C)` (a constant presheaf on the
393	irreducible scheme `C.left`), and the per-prime-divisor order conditions
394	are stalk-local in the open.
395	
396	Per analogist `ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md` Decision 3
397	(`ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`), the route is `Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`
398	applied to `CategoryTheory.forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`, mirroring the
399	project's `toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf` template
400	(`Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/SheafProperty.lean:32-37`).
401	
402	iter-187 Step 4 lands the *structural* lemma; the body of the
403	`Type`-valued sheaf condition (after the `isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`
404	reduction) is a named typed `sorry` for the iter-187 prover round
405	(per the directive's "up to one narrow named typed sorry" allowance —
406	the gluing argument on the irreducible scheme `C.left` is ~30-50 LOC
407	of explicit `Presheaf.IsSheaf`-unfolding which Mathlib doesn't ship as
408	a one-liner for sub-presheaves of constants). -/
409	private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf_isSheaf
410	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
411	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
412	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
413	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
414	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
415	    Presheaf.IsSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
416	      (lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf P hPcoh) := by
417	  rw [Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget _ _
418	        (CategoryTheory.forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
419	  -- After the forget reduction, the goal is the `Type`-valued sheaf
420	  -- condition for `U ↦ ↥(carrierSubmodule P hPcoh U)`. Since each
421	  -- `carrierSubmodule U` is a subset of `C.left.functionField` and
422	  -- `C.left` is irreducible (from `IsIntegral C.left`), matching
423	  -- sections on a cover have a unique gluing in `K(C)`; the per-prime-
424	  -- divisor order conditions on `U` follow from the conditions on
425	  -- each cover element by stalk-locality. The full unfolding is
426	  -- ~30-50 LOC of explicit `Presheaf.IsSheaf`-arithmetic, deferred

540

542	`ℒ(D_0)` with the rational functions `f ∈ K(X)^×` satisfying
543	`div(f) ≥ −D_0` (plus the zero section). Specialised to `D_0 = [P]` on a
544	curve, the condition `div(f) + [P] ≥ 0` rewrites coordinate-wise as
545	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`. -/

547	/-- **Forward direction of `globalSections_iff` (Hartshorne II.7.7(b)).**
548	
549	Given `f ∈ K(C)^×` with `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P`
550	and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`, the rational function `f` lifts to a global section
551	`s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under the canonical inclusion
552	`𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f`. Hartshorne's construction inside the
553	proof of Proposition 7.7(b) (p. 157) defines this section locally on the
554	affine cover witnessing the Cartier-divisor structure of `[P]`: on a
555	neighbourhood `U_i` of `P` the section is `f · f_P` (in
556	`𝒪_C(P)(U_i) = f_P⁻¹ · 𝒪_C(U_i)`, this is `(f · f_P) · f_P⁻¹ = f`);
557	on the complement `C ∖ {P}` the section is `f` directly (the order
558	conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` guarantee `f ∈ 𝒪_C(U) = 𝒪_C(P)(U)`
559	on any affine open `U ⊆ C ∖ {P}`).

561	**iter-187 closure**: now that `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` has its

566	the order conditions `_hord` express exactly that `f ∈ carrierSubmodule

570	`constantSheafAdj.homEquiv.symm` into a sheaf morphism, then through

572	round-trip `toFunctionField s = f` follows from the unit/counit
573	equation `adj.homEquiv_unit` evaluated at the input `(1 : kbar)`. -/

591	    refine ⟨fun Q _ hQP => _hord.1 Q hQP, fun _ => _hord.2⟩
592	  set F := lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP hPcoh
593	  let φ : kbar →ₗ[kbar] ↑(lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule P hPcoh
594	      (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) :=
595	    LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ ⟨f, hf_in⟩

597	      (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)) :=
598	    ModuleCat.ofHom φ

600	    (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) hT)
601	  let sheafHom := (adj.homEquiv (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar) F).symm φ_cat

604	  refine ⟨s, ?_⟩

606	    ((Scheme.HModule_zero_linearEquiv kbar F) s)).app
607	    (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))).hom
608	      ((adj.unit.app (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).hom (1 : kbar))).1 = f
609	  have h1 : (Scheme.HModule_zero_linearEquiv kbar F) s = sheafHom := by simp [s]

614	  rw [h_simp] at h3

618	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)) =

620	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj
621	          (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))).map sheafHom :=
622	    rfl
623	  rw [hrew]
624	  -- Use `h3` and `ModuleCat.comp_apply` to identify the goal's chain with `φ_cat.hom 1`.
625	  have h_eq : (((CategoryTheory.sheafSections (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
626	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj
627	          (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))).map sheafHom).hom
628	        ((adj.unit.app (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).hom (1 : kbar)) = φ_cat.hom 1 := by
629	    rw [h3]; exact (ModuleCat.comp_apply _ _ _).symm
630	  -- Reduce to `(φ_cat.hom 1).1 = f` via the equation `h_eq`.

641	Given a global section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under
642	`𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f`, the order conditions on `f` follow by
643	reading off the stalk-by-stalk DVR identification. Concretely:
644	
645	* At a prime divisor `Q ≠ P`, the stalk `𝒪_C(P)_Q = 𝒪_{C, Q}` agrees with
646	  the structure sheaf (since `𝒪_C(P)` equals `𝒪_C` on the open
647	  complement `C ∖ {P}`); the germ of `s` at `Q` lies in `𝒪_{C, Q}`, so

649	* At `P`, the stalk `𝒪_C(P)_P = f_P⁻¹ · 𝒪_{C, P}` (where `f_P` is a
650	  uniformiser of the DVR `𝒪_{C, P}`); the germ of `s` at `P` lies in
651	  this stalk, so `f = ι(s)` satisfies `f_P · f ∈ 𝒪_{C, P}`, i.e.
652	  `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.

679	      (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ f := by

699	/-- **Global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` as rational functions with controlled
700	pole at `P`** (Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7, p. 157).
701	
702	For a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×`, the following are
703	equivalent:
704	
705	* there exists a global section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under
706	  the canonical inclusion `𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f` (formally,
707	  `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField P hP s = f`);
708	* the order conditions hold: `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
709	  `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose generic point is not `P`, and
710	  `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (where the latter is read off the prime divisor
711	  `⟨P, h⟩` with `h : Order.coheight P = 1` the codimension-one witness
712	  automatic for a closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme).
713	
714	The iff is the substantive content of Hartshorne's Proposition 7.7(b) /
715	its proof, specialised to `D_0 = [P]`.
716	
717	**iter-181 Lane A PARTIAL — directional split landed**: the iff is now
718	proved by combining the two directional helpers

720	`globalSections_iff_mpr` (Hartshorne II.7.7(a), backward), both of

730	of `𝒦_C` generated locally by `f_P⁻¹` near `P` and by `1` elsewhere,
731	then read off the order conditions at each stalk via the DVR valuation
732	identification.

734	Blueprint reference: `lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff`
735	(Hartshorne II.7 Proposition 7.7(b), p. 157). -/
736	lemma globalSections_iff
737	    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
738	        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
739	    (P : C.left) (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
740	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)

753	/-! ## §3. Cohomological vanishing in genus zero
754	
755	Specialise Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's inductive step at `D = 0`. The
756	standard short exact sequence
757	`0 → 𝒪_C(−[P]) → 𝒪_C → k(P) → 0` (Hartshorne II.6.18: the ideal sheaf
758	of the locally principal closed subscheme `P` is `𝒪_C(−[P])`; the
759	quotient is the skyscraper `k(P) ≅ k̄` at `P`) tensored by the locally
760	free rank-`1` sheaf `𝒪_C([P])` (left rigid, so preserves exactness and
761	leaves the skyscraper invariant) becomes
762	`0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0` in `Coh(C)`. The associated long exact
763	sequence of sheaf cohomology, combined with `H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = 0`
764	(genus-`0` hypothesis: `g(C) = dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`) and
765	`H¹(C, k(P)) = 0` (skyscraper / flasque), kills `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P))`. -/
766	
767	/-- **Vanishing of `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P))` on a smooth proper geometrically
768	irreducible curve of genus `0`** (Hartshorne IV §1 p. 296, the
769	inductive step of Theorem 1.3 specialised to `D = 0`).
770	
771	Concretely, the finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector space
772	`Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP) 1` has dimension
773	`0`, i.e. is the trivial vector space.
774	
775	iter-177+ body: assemble the closed-point short exact sequence
776	`0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0`, feed it to the long exact sequence of
777	`Module k̄`-flavoured cohomology (the project's
778	`Scheme.HModule k̄`-bridge inherits the LES by forget-functor
779	naturality from
780	`CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence_exact`), substitute
781	`H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = 0` (the genus-`0` hypothesis, unfolding

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	% --- iter-187 plan-phase additions: pins for the iter-187 refactor's
102	% Step 3 + Step 4 sub-helpers (carrierPresheaf functor + sheaf property)
103	% which are the direct-construction substrate the definition below

105	% Decision 3 + Decision 4 recipe). The Lean declarations are produced

110	\begin{definition}
111	\leanok
112	[Presheaf carrier for the line bundle of a closed point]
113	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf}
115	  For each open \(U \subseteq C\), the \(\bar k\)-submodule
116	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \subset K(C)\) of section candidates
117	  satisfying the order conditions ``\(\mathrm{ord}_Q f \ge 0\) for all
118	  prime divisors \(Q \in U\) other than \(P\), and additionally
119	  \(\mathrm{ord}_P f \ge -1\) when \(P \in U\)'' assembles into a
120	  \(\bar k\)-linear functor
121	  \[
122	    \texttt{carrierPresheaf}_P : (\Opens\, |C|)^{\op}
123	    \longrightarrow \ModuleCat_{\bar k},
124	    \qquad U \longmapsto \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U),
125	  \]
126	  with restriction \(V \subseteq U \mapsto \texttt{Submodule.inclusion}\,
127	  (\texttt{carrierSet\_mono})\) on each pair. The functor laws follow from
128	  the antitone monotonicity \(U \mapsto \mathrm{carrierSet}_P(U)\) (witness
129	  \(\texttt{carrierSet\_mono}\) at L181-192 of the Lean file) by
130	  \(\texttt{ext}\,\langle x, \_\rangle\); \texttt{rfl}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{lemma}
134	\leanok
135	[Sheaf property of the carrier presheaf]
136	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
138	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
139	  The presheaf \texttt{carrierPresheaf}\(_P\) is a sheaf in the
140	  appropriate Grothendieck topology on \(C\). Concretely, the locality
141	  + gluing conditions for \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued sections reduce
142	  (via \(\texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheaf\_forget}\)) to the sheaf condition
143	  on the underlying Set-valued functor: each \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U)\)
144	  is a subset of the constant \(K(C)\)-sheaf restricted to \(U\), and
145	  the sheaf property in the constant-sheaf base case is direct. The
146	  project's \texttt{toModuleKSheaf} template
147	  (\texttt{Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/SheafProperty.lean})
148	  is the model for this argument.
149	\end{lemma}
150	
151	\begin{definition}
152	
153	
154	\leanok
155	[Line bundle of a closed point]
156	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
157	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
158	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
159	  
160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
161	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
162	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
164	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
165	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
166	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
167	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
168	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
169	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
170	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
171	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
172	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
173	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
175	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
176	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
177	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
178	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
179	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
180	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
181	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
182	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
183	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
184	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
185	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
186	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
187	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
188	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
189	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
190	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
191	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
192	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
193	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
194	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
195	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
196	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
197	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
198	
199	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
200	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
201	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
202	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
203	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
204	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
205	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
206	  \[
207	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
208	    \mathcal O_C)
209	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
210	  \]
211	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
212	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
213	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
214	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
215	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
216	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
217	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
218	
219	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
220	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
221	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
222	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
223	  REF), and returns an invertible
224	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
225	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
226	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
227	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
228	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
229	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
230	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
231	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
232	\end{definition}

234	\begin{definition}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
238	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
240	  
241	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
242	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
243	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
244	  % of the construction) (read from
245	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
247	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
248	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
249	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
250	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
251	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
252	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
253	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
254	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
255	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
256	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
257	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
258	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
259	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
260	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
261	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
262	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)
263	  produces a canonical \(\bar k\)-linear map
264	  \[
265	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
266	      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
267	  \]
268	  sending a global section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to the rational
269	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of
270	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into \(\mathcal K_C\) at each point. The map \(\iota\) is
271	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
272	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections
273	  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme \(C\)).
274	
275	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
276	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
277	  takes the same inputs as REF (a curve
278	  \(C\), a point \(P\), and the closedness witness
279	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C.left)}) and produces, for each global
280	  section \(s\) of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) at twist \(0\) in the project's
281	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured global-sections pipeline, the corresponding
282	  element \(\iota(s) \in K(C)\) of the project's function-field carrier
283	  \texttt{C.left.functionField}. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}
284	  and its body is gated on the body of
285	  REF.
286	\end{definition}

288	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
289	
290	\begin{lemma}
291	
292	
293	\leanok
294	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
295	controlled pole]
296	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
298	  
299	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
300	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
301	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
302	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
303	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
304	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
305	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
306	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
307	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
308	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
309	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
310	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
311	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
312	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
313	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
314	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
315	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
316	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
317	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
318	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
319	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
320	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
321	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
322	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
323	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
324	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
325	  Riemann--Roch space
326	  \[
327	    L([P]) \;:=\;
328	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
329	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
330	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
331	  \]
332	  Equivalently, the order conditions
333	  \[
334	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
335	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
336	    \qquad
337	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
338	  \]
339	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
340	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
341	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
342	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
343	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
344	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
345	
346	  Under this identification, the inclusion
347	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
348	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
349	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
350	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
351	\end{lemma}
352	
353	\begin{proof}
354	  
355	  
356	\leanok
357	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
358	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
359	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
360	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
361	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
362	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
363	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
364	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
365	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
366	  and only if:
367	
368	  \begin{itemize}
369	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
370	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
371	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
372	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
373	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
374	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
375	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
376	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
377	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
378	  \end{itemize}
379	  In divisorial language (REF,
380	  REF), the two conditions combine to
381	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
382	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
383	  zero global section.
384	
385	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
386	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
387	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
388	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
389	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
390	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
391	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
392	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
393	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
394	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
395	\end{proof}
