397	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
398	
399	\begin{lemma}
400	
401	
402	\leanok
403	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
404	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
406	  
407	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
408	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
409	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
410	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
411	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
412	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
414	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
415	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
416	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
417	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
418	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
419	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
420	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
421	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
422	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
423	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
424	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
425	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
426	  \[
427	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
428	  \]
429	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
430	\end{lemma}
431	
432	\begin{proof}
433	  
434	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
435	  exact sequence
436	  \[
437	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
438	  \]
439	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
440	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
441	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
442	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
443	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
444	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
445	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
446	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
447	  \[
448	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
449	  \]
450	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
451	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
452	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
453	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
454	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
455	  \begin{multline*}
456	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
457	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
458	  \end{multline*}
459	  Two ingredients close the proof:
460	
461	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
462	  hypothesis: by REF,
463	  \[
464	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
465	  \]
466	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
467	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
468	
469	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
470	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
471	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
472	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
473	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
474	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
475	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
476	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
477	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
478	
479	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
480	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
481	  \[
482	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
483	  \]
484	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
485	
486	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
487	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
488	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
489	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
490	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
491	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
492	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
493	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
494	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
495	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
496	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
497	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
498	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
499	  complex degenerates).
500	\end{proof}
501	
502	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
503	
504	\begin{theorem}
505	
506	
507	\leanok
508	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
509	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
510	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
511	  
512	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
513	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
514	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
515	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
516	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
517	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
518	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
519	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
520	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
521	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
522	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
523	  % is the Euler characteristic
524	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
525	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
526	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
527	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
528	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
529	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
530	  \[
531	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
532	  \]
533	\end{theorem}
534	
535	\begin{proof}
536	  
537	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
538	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
539	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
540	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
541	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
542	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
543	  \[
544	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
545	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
546	  \]
547	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
548	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
549	  \[
550	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
551	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
552	    \;=\; 2.
553	  \]
554	  By
555	  REF, the second
556	  term vanishes:
557	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
558	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
559	
560	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
561	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
562	  REF computes
563	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
564	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
565	  \[
566	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
567	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
568	  \]
569	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
570	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
571	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
572	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
573	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
574	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
575	  segment yields the short exact sequence
576	  \[
577	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
578	  \]
579	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
580	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
581	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
582	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
583	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
584	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
585	\end{proof}

587	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
588	
589	\begin{corollary}
590	
591	
592	\leanok
593	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
594	single simple pole]
595	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
596	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
597	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
598	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
600	  
601	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
602	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
603	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
604	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
605	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
606	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
607	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
608	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
609	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
610	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
611	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
612	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
613	  \begin{itemize}
614	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
615	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
616	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
617	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
618	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
619	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
620	  \end{itemize}
621	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
622	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
623	\end{corollary}
624	
625	\begin{proof}
626	  
627	  By
628	  REF,
629	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
630	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
631	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
632	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
633	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
634	  identification of
635	  REF), is a one-dimensional
636	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
637	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
638	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
639	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
640	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
641	
642	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
643	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
644	  REF the section
645	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
646	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
647	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
648	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
649	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
650	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
651	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
652	  of the statement are now immediate.
653	
654	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
655	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
656	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
657	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
658	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
659	
660	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
661	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
662	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
663	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
664	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
665	  REF's output. The
666	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
667	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
668	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
669	\end{proof}

671	\section{Mathlib-status flag: the H\(^1\)-vanishing input}
672	
673	The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve
674	(REF) is the
675	critical cohomological input of this chapter. Its closure depends on
676	two Mathlib-status facts, both of which the project already consumes
677	elsewhere:
678	
679	\begin{itemize}
680	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence of \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf
681	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships
682	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact} for
683	    \(\Ext^*\)-long-exact-sequences at the abelian-group level. The
684	    project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured pipeline
685	    (REF, \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex})
686	    inherits it by forget-functor naturality. This bridge is already
687	    consumed by REF of
688	    \texttt{RR.2}, so closing it here introduces no new Mathlib
689	    obligation.
690	  \item \textbf{Vanishing of higher cohomology of a skyscraper sheaf}
691	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque
692	    formulation; the project's affine-cover Čech route
693	    (REF and surrounding
694	    \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex} machinery) computes
695	    the same vanishing for the skyscraper at a closed point by direct
696	    Čech-degeneration. \texttt{RR.2}'s inductive step already invokes
697	    \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), which is the combined statement
698	    \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and \(\dim H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\),
699	    so the vanishing \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) is already a consumer
700	    obligation of \texttt{RR.2}.
701	\end{itemize}
702	
703	Consequently the H\(^1\)-vanishing
704	REF introduces no
705	Mathlib obligation that is not already on \texttt{RR.2}'s critical
706	path. The genus-\(0\) specialisation should therefore close at the
707	formalisation stage without splitting this chapter or pushing the
708	vanishing back into \texttt{RR.2}.
709	
710	% NOTE (iter-174 plan-agent flag). The general-\(g\) vanishing statement
711	% ``\(H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0\) whenever \(\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2\)''
712	% (Hartshorne~IV.1, the Riemann--Roch consequence read covariantly) is
713	% \emph{not} a deliverable of this chapter and is not required for the
714	% genus-\(0\) critical path: the genus-\(0\) argument closes via the long
715	% exact sequence at \(D = 0\) without ever needing the general bound. The
716	% general bound is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement and is queued for
717	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter together with the dualizing sheaf
718	% \(\omega_C\) and the canonical-divisor identification \(K_C \sim -2[P]\)
719	% on \(\mathbb P^1\) (Hartshorne~II.8.20.1).
720	
721	\section{Out of scope}
722	
723	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.3}
724	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters or queued for
725	follow-up iterations:
726	
727	\begin{itemize}
728	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
729	    associated to an arbitrary Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
730	    \mathrm{Div}(C)$, and the linear-equivalence isomorphism
731	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), are
732	    \emph{not} performed in this chapter. The chapter constructs only
733	    the specialisation \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) a prime divisor,
734	    which is the only case consumed by the genus-\(0\) classification
735	    chain. The general \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is queued for the
736	    post-RR-bridge follow-up (where it pairs with the general-\(g\)
737	    Riemann--Roch formula and the canonical-divisor identification).
738	  \item The general-\(g\) statement
739	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = \deg([P]) + 1 -
740	    g(C) + \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, while a direct
741	    consequence of
742	    REF of \texttt{RR.2},
743	    is not separately formalised here; the chapter restricts to the
744	    genus-\(0\) specialisation \(g = 0\) on which
745	    REF is
746	    available.
747	  \item \textbf{Serre duality} $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong
748	    H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$ and the associated
749	    canonical divisor \(K_C = c_1(\omega_C)\) are \emph{not} used at any
750	    step of this chapter and are not deliverables; the chapter routes
751	    cohomological vanishing through the long exact sequence at
752	    \(D = 0\) (REF)
753	    instead. Serre duality is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
754	  \item The morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) produced by applying
755	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} to the basis \((1, f)\) of
756	    REF,
757	    and its identification as an isomorphism, are the content of
758	    \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
759	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The present
760	    chapter supplies the basis \((1, f)\) and stops.
761	  \item The general-\(g\) vanishing
762	    \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0\) for \(\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2\) is
763	    \emph{not} part of this chapter and is not required for the
764	    genus-\(0\) critical path. It is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement
765	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
766	  \item The interpretation of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as the space
767	    of sections of a complete linear system \(|[P]|\) in
768	    projective-space form (Hartshorne~II.7.7) is gestured at by
769	    REF but the
770	    projective-space structure on the underlying set is not separately
771	    formalised; the chapter consumes only the \(\bar k\)-vector space
772	    structure on \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
773	\end{itemize}

8	/-!
9	# Sheaves of `k`-modules: the sheaf property and bundled structure sheaf
10	
11	Sub-file (2/3) of the `StructureSheafModuleK` split (refactor iter-174). This file
12	discharges the sheaf condition for `toModuleKPresheaf` (helper 7) and bundles the
13	result as `toModuleKSheaf` (helper 8); the forget-and-recover natural iso to the
14	`AddCommGrpCat`-valued structure sheaf is also packaged here. The downstream
15	finite-length carriers live in the sibling `Carriers.lean`.
16	
17	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`.

20	set_option autoImplicit false
21	
22	universe u v
23	
24	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry
25	
26	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
27	
28	variable {k : Type u} [CommRing k]
29	
30	/-- Phase A step 5 main, helper (7): the presheaf of (6) is a sheaf for the
31	Grothendieck topology of opens of `C.left.toTopCat`. -/
32	lemma toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
33	    Presheaf.IsSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
34	      (toModuleKPresheaf C) := by
35	  rw [Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget _ _ (CategoryTheory.forget (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
36	  convert (Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget _ _
37	      (CategoryTheory.forget CommRingCat.{u})).mp C.left.sheaf.property using 1
38	
39	/-- Phase A step 5 main: the structure sheaf of a `Spec k`-scheme as a sheaf
40	of `k`-modules. Bundles `toModuleKPresheaf` (helper 6) and
41	`toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf` (helper 7) into the standard `Sheaf` shape. -/
42	noncomputable def toModuleKSheaf (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
43	    Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} k) :=
44	  ⟨toModuleKPresheaf C, toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf C⟩
45	
46	/-- Phase A step 5 polish (iter-007): forget-and-recover natural iso between
47	the iter-006 `ModuleCat k`-valued structure sheaf and the iter-004
48	`AddCommGrpCat`-valued structure sheaf. The two sheaves agree on the nose at
49	the underlying-presheaf level: forgetting from `ModuleCat k` to `AddCommGrpCat`
50	via `forget₂` recovers the iter-004 `toAbSheaf C.left`. Closure body
51	`Iso.refl _`; probe-confirmed (`lean_run_code`, iter-007 plan-agent). -/
52	noncomputable def toModuleKSheaf_forgetCompare
53	    (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
54	    (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
55	        (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} k) AddCommGrpCat.{u})).obj (toModuleKSheaf C)
56	      ≅ toAbSheaf C.left :=
57	  Iso.refl _
58	
59	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

1	# Analogy: `carrierSet → Submodule → presheaf → sheaf` API alignment for `lineBundleAtClosedPoint`
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api
8	
9	## Iteration
10	185
11	
12	## Question
13	
14	The project has been blocked for ~18 iters on completing the
15	`carrierSet (Set K(C)) → carrierSubmodule (Submodule kbar K(C)) → presheaf →
16	isSheaf → Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)` chain inside
17	`RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean` for the Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` direct
18	construction of `lineBundleAtClosedPoint`. The iter-185 progress-critic
19	verdict is CHURNING + OVER_BUDGET. The directive asks four questions:
20	
21	1. Is there a Mathlib idiom for "subsheaf of `K(C)` cut out by an order
22	   condition" that the project should ALIGN to (vs. building stepwise)?
23	2. What is the right Mathlib API for the three `Submodule` closure proofs
24	   (`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`)? Is the project's `Scheme.RationalMap.order`
25	   aligned to it, or parallel to it?
26	3. Should the sheaf property go via stalk-locality (the iter-183 plan)
27	   or via the constant-sheaf-restriction `isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`
28	   pattern (the project's existing `toModuleKSheaf` template)?
29	4. Is `Scheme.RationalMap.order` (`WithZero.log ∘ Ring.ordFrac`) a
30	   parallel API to Mathlib's `IsDedekindDomain.HeightOneSpectrum.valuation`,
31	   and should the project align?
32	
33	## Project artifact(s)
34	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean:153-166` — `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet` (iter-183 axiom-clean `Set`).
35	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean:181-192` — `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono` (iter-183 monotonicity).
36	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean:224-233` — `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` (typed `sorry` body).
37	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:152-156` — `Scheme.RationalMap.order` (project's `WithZero.log ∘ Ring.ordFrac` ℤ-valued projection).
38	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:173-186` — `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` carrier class (currently exports only `Ring.KrullDimLE 1`).
39	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/SheafProperty.lean:32-44` — `toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf` + `toModuleKSheaf` (the project's existing `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`-construction template that the recipe will mirror).
40	
41	## Decisions identified
42	
43	### Decision 1 — `Subpresheaf`/`Subsheaf` API for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`-valued sheaves
44	
45	- **Mathlib idiom**: There is **no** Mathlib subsheaf API at the right
46	  level. Concretely:
47	  - `CategoryTheory.Subpresheaf` / `CategoryTheory.Subfunctor`
48	    (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Subfunctor.Basic`) is the standard idiom
49	    for "subsheaf cut out by a per-open predicate" — but it ships
50	    **`Functor C (Type w)`-only**. There is no `ModuleCat R`-valued
51	    subpresheaf machinery; `CategoryTheory.Subpresheaf` is parameterised
52	    over `F : Functor Cᵒᵖ (Type w)`, period.
53	  - `SheafOfModules.subobject` / dual / kernel functors do not ship.
54	    `SheafOfModules R` is over a sheaf-of-rings `R`, a different
55	    category from `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)` — and even there, no
56	    subobject-by-per-open-submodule API exists.
57	  - The Type-valued `Subfunctor.sheafify_isSheaf`
58	    (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Subsheaf`) computes the sheafification
59	    of a sub-presheaf but operates only on `Type`-valued targets.
60	- **Project's current path**: stepwise build (per-open
61	  `Set → Submodule → Functor → IsSheaf → Sheaf`), mirroring the
62	  project's existing `toModuleKPresheaf → toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf →
63	  toModuleKSheaf` template (`StructureSheafModuleK/{Presheaf,SheafProperty}.lean`).
64	- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent at the *direct-build* level. Mathlib
65	  ships the same building blocks (`ModuleCat.of`, `Presheaf.IsSheaf`,
66	  `Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`) but no shortcut for
67	  "subsheaf-of-`Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`-from-per-open-submodules".
68	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** with the stepwise direct build — there is no
69	  cheaper Mathlib idiom to ALIGN to here. The project's chosen
70	  `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)` carrier matches `Scheme.HModule` and is the
71	  right target; the stepwise build is canonical for this carrier.
72	
73	### Decision 2 — `Submodule` closure proofs (`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`)
74	
75	The three closure proofs needed to upgrade `carrierSet U` to a
76	`Submodule kbar (X.functionField)`:
77	
78	#### Decision 2a — Zero closure
79	
80	- **Mathlib idiom**: `WithZero.log_zero : WithZero.log (0 : WithZero (Multiplicative M)) = 0`
81	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`). Combined with the project's
82	  `order = WithZero.log ∘ ordFrac`, this gives `order Y 0 = 0` for every
83	  prime divisor `Y`. Hence `0 ≥ 0` (every off-`P` condition) and
84	  `-1 ≤ 0` (the at-`P` condition) trivially hold.
85	- **Project's current path**: not yet written.
86	- **Gap**: identical. The Mathlib lemma is one rewrite.
87	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — use `WithZero.log_zero` +
88	  `MonoidHomClass.map_zero` (already implicit in `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰`).
89	  ~5 LOC.
90	
91	#### Decision 2b — Addition closure
92	
93	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Ring.ordFrac_add`
94	  (`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Noetherian`) ships the
95	  non-archimedean inequality
96	  `min ((Ring.ordFrac R) x) ((Ring.ordFrac R) y) ≤ (Ring.ordFrac R) (x + y)`
97	  for `x + y ≠ 0` — but with the typeclass **`[IsDiscreteValuationRing R]`**,
98	  NOT `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]`. This is mathematically tight: an
99	  arbitrary Krull-dim-≤-1 Noetherian commutative ring (e.g.
100	  `ℤ[x] / (x^2)`) has `Ring.ordFrac` defined via multiplicity, which is
101	  multiplicative but NOT non-archimedean.
102	- **Project's current path**: `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` ships
103	  only `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` (WeilDivisor L173-186),
104	  not `IsDiscreteValuationRing`. So `Ring.ordFrac_add` is **NOT
105	  invocable** as-is. The project would have to either prove the
106	  non-archimedean inequality from scratch (~30-50 LOC of bespoke
107	  argument, re-deriving Hartshorne 6.2 from `Ring.ordFrac` semantics)
108	  or upgrade the class.
109	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. The cost of NOT aligning:
110	  - 30-50 LOC of bespoke add-closure proof per closure, OR
111	  - Stuck with a typed `sorry` (the iter-183 outcome).
112	  - Plus: every downstream consumer of "`order` is a valuation" will
113	    need its own bespoke bridge, multiplying the bridge-lemma debt.
114	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — strengthen
115	  `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out` to ship `IsDiscreteValuationRing
116	  (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` (and let `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derive
117	  automatically). The class is currently *misnamed/under-strength*:
118	  Hartshorne's "regular in codim 1" *means* DVR stalks at prime
119	  divisors; the `Ring.KrullDimLE 1`-only export is a strictly weaker
120	  approximation that blocks the canonical Mathlib API. ~5-10 LOC to
121	  strengthen the class + auto-derive the Krull-dim instance.
122	
123	  Once the class is upgraded, the add closure becomes ~10-15 LOC: case
124	  split on `f + g = 0` (trivial via `WithZero.log_zero`), else apply
125	  `Ring.ordFrac_add` followed by `WithZero.log` (the `WithZero.log` is
126	  monotonic on the nonzero part, so the `min` translates correctly).
127	
128	#### Decision 2c — `kbar`-scalar closure
129	
130	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Ring.ordFrac_le_smul`
131	  (`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Noetherian`) ships
132	  `(Ring.ordFrac R) f ≤ (Ring.ordFrac R) (a • f)` for `a : S` with
133	  `(algebraMap S R) a ≠ 0`, given `[Algebra S R]` and
134	  `[IsScalarTower S R K]`. For `S = kbar`, `R = X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`,
135	  `K = X.functionField`:
136	  - `[Algebra kbar (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` should be inferable from
137	    `C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))` via stalk-of-Spec-kbar-algebra.
138	  - `[IsScalarTower kbar (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField]`
139	    similarly.
140	  - For `c ≠ 0`, `algebraMap kbar (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) c` is a
141	    UNIT in the stalk (since `kbar` is a field), so by
142	    `Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit` the order is `0`, and the scalar action
143	    *preserves* (not just doesn't decrease) the order.
144	  - **NB**: `Ring.ordFrac_le_smul` works under `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]`,
145	    no DVR-upgrade required for this lemma. So the scalar closure is
146	    cheap even *before* the Decision 2b class upgrade.
147	- **Project's current path**: not yet written.
148	- **Gap**: identical at the lemma level; mild instance-synthesis work
149	  needed to expose `[Algebra kbar (stalk Y.point)]` and the scalar-tower
150	  on a `Spec kbar`-scheme.
151	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — use `Ring.ordFrac_le_smul` (or
152	  the `Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit` + `map_mul` combination for an exact
153	  equality). ~5-10 LOC plus any missing scalar-tower instance.
154	
155	### Decision 3 — Sheaf property: gluing-by-stalks vs `isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`
156	
157	- **Mathlib idiom**: `CategoryTheory.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`
158	  (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheaf`) reduces the sheaf condition for
159	  a `ModuleCat kbar`-valued presheaf to the sheaf condition for the
160	  underlying `Type`-valued presheaf (via the conservative
161	  `CategoryTheory.forget (ModuleCat kbar)`). The project's
162	  `toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf` already uses this idiom verbatim
163	  (`StructureSheafModuleK/SheafProperty.lean:32-37`).
164	
165	  For `lineBundleAtClosedPoint`, the `Type`-valued forget of the
166	  presheaf is `U ↦ ↥(carrierSubmodule U)`, a sub-Type-presheaf of the
167	  constant presheaf `U ↦ K(C)`. The constant presheaf at `K(C)` on an
168	  IRREDUCIBLE scheme `X` (which the project has via `IsIntegral C.left`)
169	  is automatically a sheaf — gluing rational functions over an open
170	  cover gives back the same rational function (a single element of the
171	  field `K(C)`). The sub-Type-presheaf inherits the sheaf condition:
172	  given matching `(fᵢ ∈ carrierSet Vᵢ)`, all `fᵢ` are the SAME
173	  `f ∈ K(C)` (forced by overlap-matching on an irreducible scheme), and
174	  `f ∈ carrierSet U` because every prime divisor `Q ∈ U` lies in some
175	  `Vᵢ`, so the per-`Q` order condition holds.
176	- **Project's current path**: iter-183 docstring (OCofP L143-144) plans
177	  "gluing-by-stalks principle (stalk-locality of the order conditions
178	  at each prime divisor)". This works but is the harder presentation —
179	  it builds the sheaf condition pointwise at every prime divisor.
180	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. The stalk-gluing route is ~80-150 LOC
181	  (iter-182 analogist's estimate; `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md` L242);
182	  the `isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget` route is ~30-50 LOC because it
183	  factors through the standing irreducibility hypothesis.
184	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — use `isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`
185	  mirroring `toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf`. The two are mathematically
186	  equivalent; the `isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget` route halves the LOC and
187	  reuses a project-side proven template.
188	
189	### Decision 4 — `Scheme.RationalMap.order` vs `IsDedekindDomain.HeightOneSpectrum.valuation`
190	
191	- **Mathlib idiom**: Two distinct APIs:
192	  - `Ring.ordFrac : K →*₀ WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)`
193	    (`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing`) — a monoid-with-zero hom
194	    parameterised by a Noetherian, Krull-dim-≤-1 commutative ring `R`,
195	    its fraction field `K`. Does NOT carry a non-archimedean inequality
196	    in general; works for any `Ring.KrullDimLE 1`.
197	  - `IsDedekindDomain.HeightOneSpectrum.valuation : K → WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)`
198	    (`Mathlib.RingTheory.DedekindDomain.AdicValuation`) — a full
199	    `Valuation` (with built-in non-archimedean inequality), parameterised
200	    by an `IsDedekindDomain R` and a height-one prime. Requires
201	    Dedekind-ness on the *global* ring `R`.
202	- **Project's current path**:
203	  `Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (stalk Y.point) f)`
204	  (WeilDivisor L152-156). This is the `WithZero.log`-projected `ℤ`-valued
205	  version of `Ring.ordFrac`, applied stalk-by-stalk.
206	- **Gap**: **NOT a parallel API**. The project's `order` aligns with
207	  `Ring.ordFrac` (the right per-stalk Krull-dim-1 entry point) plus a
208	  cosmetic ℤ-projection. It is *not* parallel to
209	  `IsDedekindDomain.HeightOneSpectrum.valuation` — that API would
210	  require an `IsDedekindDomain` instance on the whole stalk (not a
211	  per-stalk local property; cf. an integral scheme regular in codim 1
212	  whose stalks at codim-2 points are not DVRs). The project's choice
213	  of factoring through `Ring.ordFrac` is right at the granularity
214	  level: per-stalk Krull-dim-1 is the natural hypothesis the carrier
215	  class encodes, and the stalks at prime divisors are DVRs (which is
216	  Hartshorne's `(*)`).
217	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** on the API choice. The work to do is NOT a
218	  refactor of `Scheme.RationalMap.order`; it is the Decision 2b class
219	  upgrade so that the project's per-stalk Krull-dim-1 hypothesis is
220	  strengthened to DVR-ness (which is what "regular in codim 1" actually
221	  means in classical AG, and what unlocks `Ring.ordFrac_add` and the
222	  rest of the DVR-specific `Ring.ordFrac` API at
223	  `Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Noetherian`).
224	
225	## Recommendation
226	
227	### Verdict summary
228	
229	| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
230	|---|---|---|
231	| 1: Subsheaf-of-ModuleCat idiom | PROCEED | informational |
232	| 2a: Zero closure | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | major |
233	| 2b: Add closure (+ class upgrade) | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | critical |
234	| 2c: Scalar closure | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | major |
235	| 3: Sheaf property route | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | critical |
236	| 4: `RationalMap.order` API alignment | PROCEED | informational |
237	
238	The route is **not** blocked by a missing Mathlib idiom or a parallel-API
239	trap. It is blocked by a single under-strength carrier-class field
240	(`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out` exports `Ring.KrullDimLE 1`
241	instead of `IsDiscreteValuationRing`) and by the iter-183 plan choosing
242	the harder of two sheaf-property routes (stalk-gluing instead of
243	forget-to-Type-then-trivial-on-irreducible-scheme).
244	
245	### Concrete iter-186 recipe (5 steps, ~105 LOC total)
246	
247	**Step 1 — Class upgrade** (~10 LOC, edit `WeilDivisor.lean:171-186`):
248	
249	Replace the `out : ... Ring.KrullDimLE 1 ...` field with
250	`out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)`.
251	Add an instance synthesising `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` from
252	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.TFAE` /
253	`IsDedekindDomain` chain provides this — DVR ⟹ PID ⟹ Krull-dim-≤-1).
254	
255	Note: this affects the existing `Scheme.RationalMap.order` users
256	(`principal`, `principal_hom`, `principal_degree_zero`, the
257	`globalSections_iff_*` helpers, etc.), but all consumers currently take
258	`[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]` as a class hypothesis, so the
259	class-field rewrite propagates automatically without any signature change
260	downstream.
261	
262	**Step 2 — `carrierSubmodule`** (~25 LOC, new declaration after
263	`carrierSet_mono` in `OCofP.lean`):
264	
265	```lean
266	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule
267	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
268	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
269	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
270	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
271	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
272	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
273	    Submodule kbar C.left.functionField where
274	  carrier := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
275	  zero_mem' := by
276	    -- order Y 0 = WithZero.log 0 = 0; both `0 ≥ 0` and `-1 ≤ 0` hold.
277	    refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩
278	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
279	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
280	  add_mem' := by
281	    -- case-split on f + g = 0 (trivial via WithZero.log_zero), else
282	    -- apply Ring.ordFrac_add (DVR-shipped by Step 1) + WithZero.log
283	    -- monotonicity on the nonzero part.
284	    rintro f g ⟨hf₁, hf₂⟩ ⟨hg₁, hg₂⟩
285	    refine ⟨fun Q hQU hQP => ?_, fun hPU => ?_⟩
286	    · ... -- use `Ring.ordFrac_add` after the case-split
287	    · ... -- same pattern at P
288	  smul_mem' := by
289	    -- For c : kbar, algebraMap kbar (stalk) c is a unit when c ≠ 0;
290	    -- Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit gives order = 0; order Y (c • f) = order Y f.
291	    -- For c = 0, c • f = 0 reduces to zero_mem'.

{"symbol":"Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget","info":"CategoryTheory.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget.{v₁, u₁, u₂} {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁, u₁} C] {A' : Type u₂}
  [Category.{max v₁ u₁, u₂} A'] (J : GrothendieckTopology C) (P' : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A') (s : A' ⥤ Type (max v₁ u₁)) [HasLimits A']
  [PreservesLimits s] [s.ReflectsIsomorphisms] : Presheaf.IsSheaf J P' ↔ Presheaf.IsSheaf J (P' ⋙ s)
***
For a concrete category `(A, s)` where the forgetful functor `s : A ⥤ Type v` preserves limits and
reflects isomorphisms, and `A` has limits, an `A`-valued presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` is a sheaf iff its
underlying `Type`-valued presheaf `P ⋙ s : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Type` is a sheaf.

Note this lemma applies for "algebraic" categories, e.g. groups, abelian groups and rings, but not
for the category of topological spaces, topological rings, etc. since reflecting isomorphisms does
not hold.

***

12	/-!
13	# Functions satisfying a local predicate form a sheaf.
14	
15	At this stage, in `Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/SheafOfFunctions.lean`
16	we've proved that not-necessarily-continuous functions from a topological space
17	into some type (or type family) form a sheaf.
18	
19	Why do the continuous functions form a sheaf?
20	The point is just that continuity is a local condition,
21	so one can use the lifting condition for functions to provide a candidate lift,
22	then verify that the lift is actually continuous by using the factorisation condition for the lift
23	(which guarantees that on each open set it agrees with the functions being lifted,
24	which were assumed to be continuous).
25	
26	This file abstracts this argument to work for
27	any collection of dependent functions on a topological space
28	satisfying a "local predicate".
29	
30	As an application, we check that continuity is a local predicate in this sense, and provide
31	* `TopCat.sheafToTop`: continuous functions into a topological space form a sheaf
32	
33	A sheaf constructed in this way has a natural map `stalkToFiber` from the stalks
34	to the types in the ambient type family.
35	
36	We give conditions sufficient to show that this map is injective and/or surjective.
37	-/
38	
39	@[expose] public section
40	
41	noncomputable section
42	
43	variable {X : TopCat}
44	variable (T : X → Type*)
45	
46	open TopologicalSpace
47	
48	open Opposite
49	
50	open CategoryTheory
51	
52	open CategoryTheory.Limits
53	
54	open CategoryTheory.Limits.Types
55	
56	namespace TopCat
57	
58	/-- Given a topological space `X : TopCat` and a type family `T : X → Type`,
59	a `P : PrelocalPredicate T` consists of:
60	* a family of predicates `P.pred`, one for each `U : Opens X`, of the form `(Π x : U, T x) → Prop`
61	* a proof that if `f : Π x : V, T x` satisfies the predicate on `V : Opens X`, then
62	  the restriction of `f` to any open subset `U` also satisfies the predicate.
63	-/
64	structure PrelocalPredicate where
65	  /-- The underlying predicate of a prelocal predicate -/
66	  pred : ∀ {U : Opens X}, (∀ x : U, T x) → Prop
67	  /-- The underlying predicate should be invariant under restriction -/
68	  res : ∀ {U V : Opens X} (i : U ⟶ V) (f : ∀ x : V, T x) (_ : pred f), pred fun x : U ↦ f (i x)
69	
70	variable (X)
71	
72	/-- Continuity is a "prelocal" predicate on functions to a fixed topological space `T`.
73	-/
74	@[simps!]
75	def continuousPrelocal (T) [TopologicalSpace T] : PrelocalPredicate fun _ : X ↦ T where
76	  pred {_} f := Continuous f
77	  res {_ _} i _ h := Continuous.comp h (Opens.isOpenEmbedding_of_le i.le).continuous
78	
79	/-- Satisfying the inhabited linter. -/
80	instance inhabitedPrelocalPredicate (T) [TopologicalSpace T] :
81	    Inhabited (PrelocalPredicate fun _ : X ↦ T) :=
82	  ⟨continuousPrelocal X T⟩
83	
84	variable {X} in
85	/-- Given a topological space `X : TopCat` and a type family `T : X → Type`,
86	a `P : LocalPredicate T` consists of:
87	* a family of predicates `P.pred`, one for each `U : Opens X`, of the form `(Π x : U, T x) → Prop`
88	* a proof that if `f : Π x : V, T x` satisfies the predicate on `V : Opens X`, then
89	  the restriction of `f` to any open subset `U` also satisfies the predicate, and
90	* a proof that given some `f : Π x : U, T x`,
91	  if for every `x : U` we can find an open set `x ∈ V ≤ U`
92	  so that the restriction of `f` to `V` satisfies the predicate,
93	  then `f` itself satisfies the predicate.
94	-/
95	structure LocalPredicate extends PrelocalPredicate T where
96	  /-- A local predicate must be local --- provided that it is locally satisfied, it is also globally
97	  satisfied -/
98	  locality :
99	    ∀ {U : Opens X} (f : ∀ x : U, T x)
100	      (_ : ∀ x : U, ∃ (V : Opens X) (_ : x.1 ∈ V) (i : V ⟶ U),
101	        pred fun x : V ↦ f (i x : U)), pred f
102	
103	/-- Continuity is a "local" predicate on functions to a fixed topological space `T`.
104	-/
105	def continuousLocal (T) [TopologicalSpace T] : LocalPredicate fun _ : X ↦ T :=
106	  { continuousPrelocal X T with
107	    locality := fun {U} f w ↦ by
108	      apply continuous_iff_continuousAt.2
109	      intro x
110	      specialize w x
111	      rcases w with ⟨V, m, i, w⟩
112	      dsimp at w
113	      rw [continuous_iff_continuousAt] at w
114	      specialize w ⟨x, m⟩
115	      simpa using (Opens.isOpenEmbedding_of_le i.le).continuousAt_iff.1 w }
116	
117	/-- Satisfying the inhabited linter. -/
118	instance inhabitedLocalPredicate (T) [TopologicalSpace T] :
119	    Inhabited (LocalPredicate fun _ : X ↦ T) :=
120	  ⟨continuousLocal X T⟩
121	
122	variable {X T}
123	
124	/-- The conjunction of two prelocal predicates is prelocal. -/
125	def PrelocalPredicate.and (P Q : PrelocalPredicate T) : PrelocalPredicate T where
126	  pred f := P.pred f ∧ Q.pred f
127	  res i f h := ⟨P.res i f h.1, Q.res i f h.2⟩
128	
129	/-- The conjunction of two prelocal predicates is prelocal. -/
130	def LocalPredicate.and (P Q : LocalPredicate T) : LocalPredicate T where
131	  __ := P.1.and Q.1
132	  locality f w := by
133	    refine ⟨P.locality f ?_, Q.locality f ?_⟩ <;>
134	      (intro x; have ⟨V, hV, i, h⟩ := w x; use V, hV, i)
135	    exacts [h.1, h.2]
136	
137	/-- The local predicate of being a partial section of a function. -/
138	def isSection {T} (p : T → X) : LocalPredicate fun _ : X ↦ T where
139	  pred f := p ∘ f = (↑)
140	  res _ _ h := funext fun _ ↦ congr_fun h _
141	  locality _ w := funext fun x ↦ have ⟨_, hV, _, h⟩ := w x; congr_fun h ⟨x, hV⟩
142	
143	/-- Given a `P : PrelocalPredicate`, we can always construct a `LocalPredicate`
144	by asking that the condition from `P` holds locally near every point.
145	-/
146	def PrelocalPredicate.sheafify {T : X → Type*} (P : PrelocalPredicate T) : LocalPredicate T where
147	  pred {U} f := ∀ x : U, ∃ (V : Opens X) (_ : x.1 ∈ V) (i : V ⟶ U), P.pred fun x : V ↦ f (i x : U)
148	  res {V U} i f w x := by
149	    specialize w (i x)
150	    rcases w with ⟨V', m', i', p⟩
151	    exact ⟨V ⊓ V', ⟨x.2, m'⟩, V.infLELeft _, P.res (V.infLERight V') _ p⟩
152	  locality {U} f w x := by
153	    specialize w x
154	    rcases w with ⟨V, m, i, p⟩
155	    specialize p ⟨x.1, m⟩
156	    rcases p with ⟨V', m', i', p'⟩
157	    exact ⟨V', m', i' ≫ i, p'⟩
158	
159	namespace PrelocalPredicate
160	
161	theorem sheafifyOf {T : X → Type*} {P : PrelocalPredicate T} {U : Opens X}
162	    {f : ∀ x : U, T x} (h : P.pred f) : P.sheafify.pred f := fun x ↦
163	  ⟨U, x.2, 𝟙 _, by convert h⟩
164	
165	/-- For a unary operation (e.g. `x ↦ -x`) defined at each stalk, if a prelocal predicate is closed
166	under the operation on each open set (possibly by refinement), then the sheafified predicate is
167	also closed under the operation. See `sheafify_inductionOn'` for the version without refinement. -/
168	theorem sheafify_inductionOn {X : TopCat} {T : X → Type*} (P : PrelocalPredicate T)
169	    (op : {x : X} → T x → T x)
170	    (hop : ∀ {U : Opens X} {a : (x : U) → T x}, P.pred a →
171	      ∀ (p : U), ∃ (W : Opens X) (i : W ⟶ U), p.1 ∈ W ∧ P.pred fun x : W ↦ op (a (i x)))
172	    {U : Opens X} {a : (x : U) → T x} (ha : P.sheafify.pred a) :
173	    P.sheafify.pred (fun x : U ↦ op (a x)) := by
174	  intro x
175	  rcases ha x with ⟨Va, ma, ia, ha⟩
176	  rcases hop ha ⟨x, ma⟩ with ⟨W, sa, hx, hw⟩
177	  exact ⟨W, hx, sa ≫ ia, hw⟩
178	
179	/-- For a unary operation (e.g. `x ↦ -x`) defined at each stalk, if a prelocal predicate is closed
180	under the operation on each open set, then the sheafified predicate is also closed under the
181	operation. See `sheafify_inductionOn` for the version with refinement. -/
182	theorem sheafify_inductionOn' {X : TopCat} {T : X → Type*} (P : PrelocalPredicate T)
183	    (op : {x : X} → T x → T x)
184	    (hop : ∀ {U : Opens X} {a : (x : U) → T x}, P.pred a → P.pred fun x : U ↦ op (a x))
185	    {U : Opens X} {a : (x : U) → T x} (ha : P.sheafify.pred a) :
186	    P.sheafify.pred (fun x : U ↦ op (a x)) :=
187	  P.sheafify_inductionOn op (fun ha p ↦ ⟨_, 𝟙 _, p.2, hop ha⟩) ha
188	
189	/-- For a binary operation (e.g. `x ↦ y ↦ x + y`) defined at each stalk, if a prelocal predicate is
190	closed under the operation on each open set (possibly by refinement), then the sheafified predicate
191	is also closed under the operation. See `sheafify_inductionOn₂'` for the version without
192	refinement. -/
193	theorem sheafify_inductionOn₂ {X : TopCat} {T₁ T₂ T₃ : X → Type*}
194	    (P₁ : PrelocalPredicate T₁) (P₂ : PrelocalPredicate T₂) (P₃ : PrelocalPredicate T₃)
195	    (op : {x : X} → T₁ x → T₂ x → T₃ x)
196	    (hop : ∀ {U V : Opens X} {a : (x : U) → T₁ x} {b : (x : V) → T₂ x}, P₁.pred a → P₂.pred b →
197	      ∀ (p : (U ⊓ V : Opens X)), ∃ (W : Opens X) (ia : W ⟶ U) (ib : W ⟶ V),
198	      p.1 ∈ W ∧ P₃.pred fun x : W ↦ op (a (ia x)) (b (ib x)))
199	    {U : Opens X} {a : (x : U) → T₁ x} {b : (x : U) → T₂ x}
200	    (ha : P₁.sheafify.pred a) (hb : P₂.sheafify.pred b) :

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Grothendieck
9	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Pretopology
10	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.Lattice
11	public import Mathlib.Topology.Sets.Opens
12	
13	/-!
14	# Grothendieck topology on a topological space
15	
16	Define the Grothendieck topology and the pretopology associated to a topological space, and show
17	that the pretopology induces the topology.
18	
19	The covering (pre)sieves on `X` are those for which the union of domains contains `X`.
20	
21	## Tags
22	
23	site, Grothendieck topology, space
24	
25	## References
26	
27	* [nLab, *Grothendieck topology*](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Grothendieck+topology)
28	* [S. MacLane, I. Moerdijk, *Sheaves in Geometry and Logic*][MM92]
29	
30	## Implementation notes
31	
32	We define the two separately, rather than defining the Grothendieck topology as that generated
33	by the pretopology for the purpose of having nice definitional properties for the sieves.
34	-/
35	
36	@[expose] public section
37	
38	
39	universe u
40	
41	namespace Opens
42	
43	variable (T : Type u) [TopologicalSpace T]
44	
45	open CategoryTheory TopologicalSpace CategoryTheory.Limits
46	
47	/-- The Grothendieck topology associated to a topological space. -/
48	def grothendieckTopology : GrothendieckTopology (Opens T) where
49	  sieves X := {S | ∀ x ∈ X, ∃ (U : Opens T) (f : U ⟶ X), S f ∧ x ∈ U}
50	  top_mem' _ _ hx := ⟨_, 𝟙 _, trivial, hx⟩
51	  pullback_stable' X Y S f hf y hy := by
52	    rcases hf y (f.le hy) with ⟨U, g, hg, hU⟩
53	    refine ⟨U ⊓ Y, homOfLE inf_le_right, ?_, hU, hy⟩
54	    apply S.downward_closed hg (homOfLE inf_le_left)
55	  transitive' X S hS R hR x hx := by
56	    rcases hS x hx with ⟨U, f, hf, hU⟩
57	    rcases hR hf _ hU with ⟨V, g, hg, hV⟩
58	    exact ⟨_, g ≫ f, hg, hV⟩
59	
60	/-- The Grothendieck pretopology associated to a topological space. -/
61	def pretopology : Pretopology (Opens T) where
62	  coverings X := {R | ∀ x ∈ X, ∃ (U : _) (f : U ⟶ X), R f ∧ x ∈ U}
63	  has_isos _ _ f _ _ hx := ⟨_, _, Presieve.singleton_self _, (inv f).le hx⟩
64	  pullbacks X Y f S hS x hx := by
65	    rcases hS _ (f.le hx) with ⟨U, g, hg, hU⟩
66	    refine ⟨_, _, Presieve.pullbackArrows.mk _ _ hg, ?_⟩
67	    have : U ⊓ Y ≤ pullback g f :=
68	      leOfHom (pullback.lift (homOfLE inf_le_left) (homOfLE inf_le_right) rfl)
69	    apply this ⟨hU, hx⟩
70	  transitive X S Ti hS hTi x hx := by
71	    rcases hS x hx with ⟨U, f, hf, hU⟩
72	    rcases hTi f hf x hU with ⟨V, g, hg, hV⟩
73	    exact ⟨_, _, ⟨_, g, f, hf, hg, rfl⟩, hV⟩
74	
75	/-- The pretopology associated to a space is the largest pretopology that
76	generates the Grothendieck topology associated to the space. -/
77	@[simp]
78	theorem toPretopology_grothendieckTopology :
79	    (Opens.grothendieckTopology T).toPretopology = Opens.pretopology T := by
80	  apply le_antisymm
81	  · intro X R hR x hx
82	    rcases hR x hx with ⟨U, f, ⟨V, g₁, g₂, hg₂, _⟩, hU⟩
83	    exact ⟨V, g₂, hg₂, g₁.le hU⟩
84	  · intro X R hR x hx
85	    rcases hR x hx with ⟨U, f, hf, hU⟩
86	    exact ⟨U, f, Sieve.le_generate R U _ hf, hU⟩
87	
88	/-- The pretopology associated to a space induces the Grothendieck topology associated to the space.
89	-/
90	@[simp]
91	theorem pretopology_toGrothendieck :
92	    (Opens.pretopology T).toGrothendieck = Opens.grothendieckTopology T := by
93	  rw [← toPretopology_grothendieckTopology]
94	  apply (Pretopology.gi (Opens T)).l_u_eq
95	
96	end Opens
97

{"symbol":"Presheaf.IsSheaf","info":"CategoryTheory.Presheaf.IsSheaf.{v₁, v₂, u₁, u₂} {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁, u₁} C] {A : Type u₂} [Category.{v₂, u₂} A]
  (J : GrothendieckTopology C) (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) : Prop
***
A sheaf of A is a presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` such that for every `E : A`, the
presheaf of types given by sending `U : C` to `Hom_{A}(E, P U)` is a sheaf of types. 
***

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2020 Kevin Buzzard, Bhavik Mehta. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Kevin Buzzard, Bhavik Mehta
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.Preserves.Shapes.Equalizers
9	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.Preserves.Shapes.Products
10	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.Yoneda
11	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Preadditive.FunctorCategory
12	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.SheafOfTypes
13	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.EqualizerSheafCondition
14	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.Constructions.EpiMono
15	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Shapes.Terminal
16	
17	/-!
18	# Sheaves taking values in a category
19	
20	If C is a category with a Grothendieck topology, we define the notion of a sheaf taking values in
21	an arbitrary category `A`. We follow the definition in https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00VR,
22	noting that the presheaf of sets "defined above" can be seen in the comments between tags 00VQ and
23	00VR on the page <https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00VL>. The advantage of this definition is
24	that we need no assumptions whatsoever on `A` other than the assumption that the morphisms in `C`
25	and `A` live in the same universe.
26	
27	* An `A`-valued presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` is defined to be a sheaf (for the topology `J`) iff for
28	  every `E : A`, the type-valued presheaves of sets given by sending `U : Cᵒᵖ` to `Hom_{A}(E, P U)`
29	  are all sheaves of sets, see `CategoryTheory.Presheaf.IsSheaf`.
30	* When `A = Type`, this recovers the basic definition of sheaves of sets, see
31	  `CategoryTheory.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_of_type`.
32	* An alternate definition in terms of limits, unconditionally equivalent to the original one:
33	  see `CategoryTheory.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isLimit`.
34	* An alternate definition when `C` is small, has pullbacks and `A` has products is given by an
35	  equalizer condition `CategoryTheory.Presheaf.IsSheaf'`. This is equivalent to the earlier
36	  definition, shown in `CategoryTheory.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf'`.
37	* When `A = Type`, this is *definitionally* equal to the equalizer condition for presieves in
38	  `CategoryTheory.Sites.SheafOfTypes`.
39	* When `A` has limits and there is a functor `s : A ⥤ Type` which is faithful, reflects isomorphisms
40	  and preserves limits, then `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` is a sheaf iff the underlying presheaf of types
41	  `P ⋙ s : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Type` is a sheaf (`CategoryTheory.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`).
42	  Cf https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0073, which is a weaker version of this statement (it's
43	  only over spaces, not sites) and https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00YR (a), which
44	  additionally assumes filtered colimits.
45	
46	## Implementation notes
47	
48	Occasionally we need to take a limit in `A` of a collection of morphisms of `C` indexed
49	by a collection of objects in `C`. This turns out to force the morphisms of `A` to be
50	in a sufficiently large universe. Rather than use `UnivLE` we prove some results for
51	a category `A'` instead, whose morphism universe of `A'` is defined to be `max u₁ v₁`, where
52	`u₁, v₁` are the universes for `C`. Perhaps after we get better at handling universe
53	inequalities this can be changed.
54	
55	-/
56	
57	@[expose] public section
58	
59	
60	universe w v₁ v₂ v₃ u₁ u₂ u₃
61	
62	noncomputable section
63	
64	namespace CategoryTheory
65	
66	open Opposite CategoryTheory Category Limits Sieve
67	
68	namespace Presheaf
69	
70	variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C]
71	variable {A : Type u₂} [Category.{v₂} A]
72	variable (J : GrothendieckTopology C)
73	
74	-- We follow https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00VL definition 00VR
75	/-- A sheaf of A is a presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` such that for every `E : A`, the
76	presheaf of types given by sending `U : C` to `Hom_{A}(E, P U)` is a sheaf of types. -/
77	@[stacks 00VR]
78	def IsSheaf (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) : Prop :=
79	  ∀ E : A, Presieve.IsSheaf J (P ⋙ coyoneda.obj (op E))
80	
81	/-- Condition that a presheaf with values in a concrete category is separated for
82	a Grothendieck topology. -/
83	def IsSeparated (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) {FA : A → A → Type*} {CA : A → Type*}
84	    [∀ X Y, FunLike (FA X Y) (CA X) (CA Y)] [ConcreteCategory A FA] : Prop :=
85	  ∀ (X : C) (S : Sieve X) (_ : S ∈ J X) (x y : ToType (P.obj (op X))),
86	    (∀ (Y : C) (f : Y ⟶ X) (_ : S f), P.map f.op x = P.map f.op y) → x = y
87	
88	section LimitSheafCondition
89	
90	open Presieve Presieve.FamilyOfElements Limits
91	
92	variable (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) {X : C} (S : Sieve X) (R : Presieve X) (E : Aᵒᵖ)
93	
94	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
95	/-- Given a sieve `S` on `X : C`, a presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A`, and an object `E` of `A`,
96	    the cones over the natural diagram `S.arrows.diagram.op ⋙ P` associated to `S` and `P`
97	    with cone point `E` are in 1-1 correspondence with `SieveCompatible` family of elements
98	    for the sieve `S` and the presheaf of types `Hom (E, P -)`. -/
99	def conesEquivSieveCompatibleFamily :
100	    (S.arrows.diagram.op ⋙ P).cones.obj E ≃
101	      { x : FamilyOfElements (P ⋙ coyoneda.obj E) (S : Presieve X) // x.SieveCompatible } where
102	  toFun π :=
103	    ⟨fun _ f h => π.app (op ⟨Over.mk f, h⟩), fun X Y f g hf => by
104	      let φ : S.arrows.categoryMk (g ≫ f) (S.downward_closed hf g) ⟶
105	        S.arrows.categoryMk f hf := ObjectProperty.homMk (Over.homMk _ (by rfl))
106	      simpa using π.naturality φ.op⟩
107	  invFun x :=
108	    { app := fun f => x.1 f.unop.1.hom f.unop.2
109	      naturality := fun f f' g => by
110	        have := x.2 f.unop.1.hom g.unop.hom.left f.unop.2
111	        dsimp at this ⊢
112	        rw [id_comp, ← this]
113	        convert rfl
114	        simp only [Over.w] }
115	
116	variable {P S E}
117	variable {x : FamilyOfElements (P ⋙ coyoneda.obj E) S.arrows} (hx : SieveCompatible x)
118	
119	/-- The cone corresponding to a `SieveCompatible` family of elements, dot notation enabled. -/
120	@[simp]
121	def _root_.CategoryTheory.Presieve.FamilyOfElements.SieveCompatible.cone :
122	    Cone (S.arrows.diagram.op ⋙ P) where
123	  pt := E.unop
124	  π := (conesEquivSieveCompatibleFamily P S E).invFun ⟨x, hx⟩
125	
126	/-- Cone morphisms from the cone corresponding to a `SieveCompatible` family to the natural
127	    cone associated to a sieve `S` and a presheaf `P` are in 1-1 correspondence with amalgamations
128	    of the family. -/
129	def homEquivAmalgamation :
130	    (hx.cone ⟶ P.mapCone S.arrows.cocone.op) ≃ { t // x.IsAmalgamation t } where
131	  toFun l := ⟨l.hom, fun _ f hf => l.w (op ⟨Over.mk f, hf⟩)⟩
132	  invFun t := ⟨t.1, fun f => t.2 f.unop.1.hom f.unop.2⟩
133	
134	variable (P S)
135	
136	/-- Given sieve `S` and presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A`, their natural associated cone is a limit cone
137	    iff `Hom (E, P -)` is a sheaf of types for the sieve `S` and all `E : A`. -/
138	theorem isLimit_iff_isSheafFor :
139	    Nonempty (IsLimit (P.mapCone S.arrows.cocone.op)) ↔
140	      ∀ E : Aᵒᵖ, IsSheafFor (P ⋙ coyoneda.obj E) S.arrows := by
141	  dsimp [IsSheafFor]; simp_rw [compatible_iff_sieveCompatible]
142	  rw [((Cone.isLimitEquivIsTerminal _).trans (isTerminalEquivUnique _ _)).nonempty_congr]
143	  rw [Classical.nonempty_pi]; constructor
144	  · intro hu E x hx
145	    specialize hu hx.cone
146	    rw [(homEquivAmalgamation hx).uniqueCongr.nonempty_congr] at hu
147	    exact (unique_subtype_iff_existsUnique _).1 hu
148	  · rintro h ⟨E, π⟩
149	    let eqv := conesEquivSieveCompatibleFamily P S (op E)
150	    rw [← eqv.left_inv π]
151	    erw [(homEquivAmalgamation (eqv π).2).uniqueCongr.nonempty_congr]
152	    rw [unique_subtype_iff_existsUnique]
153	    exact h _ _ (eqv π).2
154	
155	/-- Given sieve `S` and presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A`, their natural associated cone admits at most one
156	    morphism from every cone in the same category (i.e. over the same diagram),
157	    iff `Hom (E, P -)` is separated for the sieve `S` and all `E : A`. -/
158	theorem subsingleton_iff_isSeparatedFor :
159	    (∀ c, Subsingleton (c ⟶ P.mapCone S.arrows.cocone.op)) ↔
160	      ∀ E : Aᵒᵖ, IsSeparatedFor (P ⋙ coyoneda.obj E) S.arrows := by
161	  constructor
162	  · intro hs E x t₁ t₂ h₁ h₂
163	    have hx := is_compatible_of_exists_amalgamation x ⟨t₁, h₁⟩
164	    rw [compatible_iff_sieveCompatible] at hx
165	    specialize hs hx.cone
166	    rcases hs with ⟨hs⟩
167	    simpa only [Subtype.mk.injEq] using (show Subtype.mk t₁ h₁ = ⟨t₂, h₂⟩ from
168	      (homEquivAmalgamation hx).symm.injective (hs _ _))
169	  · rintro h ⟨E, π⟩
170	    let eqv := conesEquivSieveCompatibleFamily P S (op E)
171	    constructor
172	    rw [← eqv.left_inv π]
173	    intro f₁ f₂
174	    let eqv' := homEquivAmalgamation (eqv π).2
175	    apply eqv'.injective
176	    ext
177	    apply h _ (eqv π).1 <;> exact (eqv' _).2
178	
179	/-- A presheaf `P` is a sheaf for the Grothendieck topology `J` iff for every covering sieve
180	    `S` of `J`, the natural cone associated to `P` and `S` is a limit cone. -/
181	theorem isSheaf_iff_isLimit :
182	    IsSheaf J P ↔

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Forget
9	public import Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.PairwiseIntersections
10	
11	/-!
12	# The sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings
13	
14	We provide an alternative formulation of the sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings.
15	
16	We work with sheaves valued in a concrete category `C` admitting all limits, whose forgetful
17	functor `C ⥤ Type` preserves limits and reflects isomorphisms. The usual categories of algebraic
18	structures, such as `MonCat`, `AddCommGrpCat`, `RingCat`, `CommRingCat` etc. are all examples of
19	this kind of category.
20	
21	A presheaf `F : Presheaf C X` satisfies the sheaf condition if and only if, for every
22	compatible family of sections `sf : Π i : ι, F.obj (op (U i))`, there exists a unique gluing
23	`s : F.obj (op (iSup U))`.
24	
25	Here, the family `sf` is called compatible, if for all `i j : ι`, the restrictions of `sf i`
26	and `sf j` to `U i ⊓ U j` agree. A section `s : F.obj (op (iSup U))` is a gluing for the
27	family `sf`, if `s` restricts to `sf i` on `U i` for all `i : ι`
28	
29	We show that the sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings is equivalent to the definition
30	in terms of pairwise intersections. Our approach is as follows: First, we show them to be equivalent
31	for `Type`-valued presheaves. Then we use that composing a presheaf with a limit-preserving and
32	isomorphism-reflecting functor leaves the sheaf condition invariant, as shown in
33	`Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Forget.lean`.
34	
35	-/
36	
37	@[expose] public section
38	
39	noncomputable section
40	
41	open TopCat TopCat.Presheaf CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits
42	  TopologicalSpace TopologicalSpace.Opens Opposite
43	
44	universe x
45	
46	variable {C : Type*} [Category* C] {FC : C → C → Type*} {CC : C → Type*}
47	variable [∀ X Y, FunLike (FC X Y) (CC X) (CC Y)] [ConcreteCategory C FC]
48	
49	namespace TopCat
50	
51	namespace Presheaf
52	
53	section
54	
55	variable {X : TopCat.{x}} (F : Presheaf C X) {ι : Type*} (U : ι → Opens X)
56	
57	/-- A family of sections `sf` is compatible, if the restrictions of `sf i` and `sf j` to `U i ⊓ U j`
58	agree, for all `i` and `j`
59	-/
60	def IsCompatible (sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.obj (op (U i)))) : Prop :=
61	  ∀ i j : ι, F.map (infLELeft (U i) (U j)).op (sf i) = F.map (infLERight (U i) (U j)).op (sf j)
62	
63	/-- A section `s` is a gluing for a family of sections `sf` if it restricts to `sf i` on `U i`,
64	for all `i`
65	-/
66	def IsGluing (sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.obj (op (U i)))) (s : ToType (F.obj (op (iSup U)))) : Prop :=
67	  ∀ i : ι, F.map (Opens.leSupr U i).op s = sf i
68	
69	/--
70	The sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings. A presheaf `F : Presheaf C X` satisfies this sheaf
71	condition if and only if, for every compatible family of sections `sf : Π i : ι, F.obj (op (U i))`,
72	there exists a unique gluing `s : F.obj (op (iSup U))`.
73	
74	We prove this to be equivalent to the usual one below in
75	`TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing`
76	-/
77	def IsSheafUniqueGluing : Prop :=
78	  ∀ ⦃ι : Type x⦄ (U : ι → Opens X) (sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.obj (op (U i)))),
79	    IsCompatible F U sf → ∃! s : ToType (F.obj (op (iSup U))), IsGluing F U sf s
80	
81	end
82	
83	section TypeValued
84	
85	variable {X : TopCat.{x}} {F : Presheaf Type* X} {ι : Type*} {U : ι → Opens X}
86	
87	/-- Given sections over a family of open sets, extend it to include
88	  sections over pairwise intersections of the open sets. -/
89	def objPairwiseOfFamily (sf : ∀ i, F.obj (op (U i))) :
90	    ∀ i, ((Pairwise.diagram U).op ⋙ F).obj i
91	  | ⟨Pairwise.single i⟩ => sf i
92	  | ⟨Pairwise.pair i j⟩ => F.map (infLELeft (U i) (U j)).op (sf i)
93	
94	/-- Given a compatible family of sections over open sets, extend it to a
95	  section of the functor `(Pairwise.diagram U).op ⋙ F`. -/
96	def IsCompatible.sectionPairwise {sf} (h : IsCompatible F U sf) :
97	    ((Pairwise.diagram U).op ⋙ F).sections := by
98	  refine ⟨objPairwiseOfFamily sf, ?_⟩
99	  let G := (Pairwise.diagram U).op ⋙ F
100	  rintro (i | ⟨i, j⟩) (i' | ⟨i', j'⟩) (_ | _ | _ | _)
101	  · exact ConcreteCategory.congr_hom (G.map_id <| op <| Pairwise.single i) _
102	  · rfl
103	  · exact (h i' i).symm
104	  · exact ConcreteCategory.congr_hom (G.map_id <| op <| Pairwise.pair i j) _
105	
106	theorem isGluing_iff_pairwise {sf s} : IsGluing F U sf s ↔
107	    ∀ i, (F.mapCone (Pairwise.cocone U).op).π.app i s = objPairwiseOfFamily sf i := by
108	  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun h i ↦ h (op <| Pairwise.single i)⟩
109	  rintro (i | ⟨i, j⟩)
110	  · exact h i
111	  · rw [← (F.mapCone (Pairwise.cocone U).op).w (op <| Pairwise.Hom.left i j)]
112	    exact congr_arg _ (h i)
113	
114	theorem IsSheaf.isSheafUniqueGluing_types (h : F.IsSheaf) (sf : ∀ i : ι, F.obj (op (U i)))
115	    (cpt : IsCompatible F U sf) : ∃! s : F.obj (op (iSup U)), IsGluing F U sf s := by
116	  simp_rw [isGluing_iff_pairwise]
117	  exact (Types.isLimit_iff _).mp (h.isSheafPairwiseIntersections U) _ cpt.sectionPairwise.prop
118	
119	variable (F)
120	
121	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
122	/-- For type-valued presheaves, the sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings is equivalent to the
123	usual sheaf condition.
124	-/
125	theorem isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing_types : F.IsSheaf ↔ F.IsSheafUniqueGluing := by
126	  simp_rw [isSheaf_iff_isSheafPairwiseIntersections, IsSheafPairwiseIntersections,
127	    Types.isLimit_iff, IsSheafUniqueGluing, isGluing_iff_pairwise]
128	  refine forall₂_congr fun ι U ↦ ⟨fun h sf cpt ↦ ?_, fun h s hs ↦ ?_⟩
129	  · exact h _ cpt.sectionPairwise.prop
130	  · specialize h (fun i ↦ s <| op <| Pairwise.single i) fun i j ↦
131	      (hs <| op <| Pairwise.Hom.left i j).trans (hs <| op <| Pairwise.Hom.right i j).symm
132	    convert h; ext (i | ⟨i, j⟩)
133	    · rfl
134	    · exact (hs <| op <| Pairwise.Hom.left i j).symm
135	
136	/-- The usual sheaf condition can be obtained from the sheaf condition
137	in terms of unique gluings.
138	-/
139	theorem isSheaf_of_isSheafUniqueGluing_types (Fsh : F.IsSheafUniqueGluing) : F.IsSheaf :=
140	  (isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing_types F).mpr Fsh
141	
142	end TypeValued
143	
144	section
145	
146	variable [HasLimitsOfSize.{x, x} C] [(forget C).ReflectsIsomorphisms]
147	  [PreservesLimitsOfSize.{x, x} (forget C)]
148	variable {X : TopCat.{x}} {F : Presheaf C X}
149	
150	theorem IsSheaf.isSheafUniqueGluing (h : F.IsSheaf) {ι : Type*} (U : ι → Opens X)
151	    (sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.obj (op (U i))))
152	    (cpt : IsCompatible F U sf) : ∃! s : ToType (F.obj (op (iSup U))), IsGluing F U sf s :=
153	  ((isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_comp' (forget C) F).mp h).isSheafUniqueGluing_types sf cpt
154	
155	variable (F)
156	
157	/-- For presheaves valued in a concrete category, whose forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms and
158	preserves limits, the sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings is equivalent to the usual one.
159	-/
160	theorem isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing : F.IsSheaf ↔ F.IsSheafUniqueGluing :=
161	  Iff.trans (isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_comp' (forget C) F)
162	    (isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing_types (F ⋙ forget C))
163	
164	end
165	
166	end Presheaf

174	variable [HasLimitsOfSize.{x, x} C] [(CategoryTheory.forget C).ReflectsIsomorphisms]
175	variable [PreservesLimitsOfSize.{x, x} (CategoryTheory.forget C)]
176	variable {X : TopCat.{x}} (F : Sheaf C X) {ι : Type*} (U : ι → Opens X)
177	
178	/-- A more convenient way of obtaining a unique gluing of sections for a sheaf.
179	-/
180	theorem existsUnique_gluing (sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.1.obj (op (U i))))
181	    (h : IsCompatible F.1 U sf) :
182	    ∃! s : ToType (F.1.obj (op (iSup U))), IsGluing F.1 U sf s :=
183	  IsSheaf.isSheafUniqueGluing F.property U sf h
184	
185	/-- In this version of the lemma, the inclusion homs `iUV` can be specified directly by the user,
186	which can be more convenient in practice.
187	-/
188	theorem existsUnique_gluing' (V : Opens X) (iUV : ∀ i : ι, U i ⟶ V) (hcover : V ≤ iSup U)
189	    (sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.1.obj (op (U i)))) (h : IsCompatible F.1 U sf) :
190	    ∃! s : ToType (F.1.obj (op V)), ∀ i : ι, F.1.map (iUV i).op s = sf i := by
191	  have V_eq_supr_U : V = iSup U := le_antisymm hcover (iSup_le fun i => (iUV i).le)
192	  obtain ⟨gl, gl_spec, gl_uniq⟩ := F.existsUnique_gluing U sf h
193	  refine ⟨F.1.map (eqToHom V_eq_supr_U).op gl, ?_, ?_⟩
194	  · intro i
195	    rw [← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← F.1.map_comp]
196	    exact gl_spec i
197	  · intro gl' gl'_spec
198	    convert congr_arg _ (gl_uniq (F.1.map (eqToHom V_eq_supr_U.symm).op gl') fun i => _) <;>
199	      rw [← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← F.1.map_comp]
200	    · simp
201	    · exact gl'_spec i
202	
203	@[ext]
204	theorem eq_of_locally_eq (s t : ToType (F.1.obj (op (iSup U))))
205	    (h : ∀ i, F.1.map (Opens.leSupr U i).op s = F.1.map (Opens.leSupr U i).op t) : s = t := by
206	  let sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.1.obj (op (U i))) := fun i => F.1.map (Opens.leSupr U i).op s
207	  have sf_compatible : IsCompatible _ U sf := by
208	    intro i j
209	    simp_rw [sf, ← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← F.1.map_comp]
210	    rfl
211	  obtain ⟨gl, -, gl_uniq⟩ := F.existsUnique_gluing U sf sf_compatible
212	  trans gl
213	  · apply gl_uniq
214	    intro i
215	    rfl
216	  · symm
217	    apply gl_uniq
218	    intro i
219	    rw [← h]
220	
221	/-- In this version of the lemma, the inclusion homs `iUV` can be specified directly by the user,
222	which can be more convenient in practice.
223	-/
224	theorem eq_of_locally_eq' (V : Opens X) (iUV : ∀ i : ι, U i ⟶ V) (hcover : V ≤ iSup U)
225	    (s t : ToType (F.1.obj (op V))) (h : ∀ i, F.1.map (iUV i).op s = F.1.map (iUV i).op t) :
226	    s = t := by
227	  have V_eq_supr_U : V = iSup U := le_antisymm hcover (iSup_le fun i => (iUV i).le)
228	  suffices F.1.map (eqToHom V_eq_supr_U.symm).op s = F.1.map (eqToHom V_eq_supr_U.symm).op t by
229	    convert congr_arg (F.1.map (eqToHom V_eq_supr_U).op) this <;>
230	    rw [← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← F.1.map_comp, eqToHom_op, eqToHom_op, eqToHom_trans,
231	      eqToHom_refl, F.1.map_id, ConcreteCategory.id_apply]
232	  apply eq_of_locally_eq
233	  intro i
234	  rw [← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← F.1.map_comp]
235	  exact h i
236	
237	theorem eq_of_locally_eq₂ {U₁ U₂ V : Opens X} (i₁ : U₁ ⟶ V) (i₂ : U₂ ⟶ V) (hcover : V ≤ U₁ ⊔ U₂)
238	    (s t : ToType (F.1.obj (op V))) (h₁ : F.1.map i₁.op s = F.1.map i₁.op t)
239	    (h₂ : F.1.map i₂.op s = F.1.map i₂.op t) : s = t := by
240	  classical
241	    fapply F.eq_of_locally_eq' fun t : Bool => if t then U₁ else U₂
242	    · exact fun i => if h : i then eqToHom (if_pos h) ≫ i₁ else eqToHom (if_neg h) ≫ i₂
243	    · refine le_trans hcover ?_
244	      rw [sup_le_iff]
245	      constructor
246	      · exact le_iSup (fun t : Bool => if t then U₁ else U₂) true
247	      · exact le_iSup (fun t : Bool => if t then U₁ else U₂) false
248	    · rintro ⟨_ | _⟩
249	      any_goals exact h₁
250	      any_goals exact h₂
251	
252	variable {F} {U} in
253	theorem eq_app_of_locally_eq {V : Opens X} {G : Sheaf C X} {f : F ⟶ G}
254	    {s : ToType (F.1.obj (op (iSup U)))} {t : ToType (G.1.obj (op V))}
255	    {sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.1.obj (op (U i)))} (h : IsGluing F.1 U sf s) (hV : ∀ i : ι, U i ≤ V)
256	    (ht : ∀ i : ι, f.1.app (op (U i)) (sf i) = G.1.map (homOfLE (hV i)).op t) :
257	    f.hom.app (op (iSup U)) s = G.obj.map (homOfLE (by aesop_cat)).op t := by
258	  refine eq_of_locally_eq G U _ _ (fun _ ↦ ?_)
259	  rw [← NatTrans.naturality_apply, h, ht, ← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← Functor.map_comp]
260	  rfl

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Presheaf
9	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheaf
10	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Spaces
11	
12	/-!
13	# Sheaves
14	
15	We define sheaves on a topological space, with values in an arbitrary category.
16	
17	A presheaf on a topological space `X` is a sheaf precisely when it is a sheaf under the
18	Grothendieck topology on `opens X`, which expands out to say: For each open cover `{ Uᵢ }` of
19	`U`, and a family of compatible functions `A ⟶ F(Uᵢ)` for an `A : X`, there exists a unique
20	gluing `A ⟶ F(U)` compatible with the restriction.
21	
22	See the docstring of `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheaf` for an explanation on the design decisions and a list
23	of equivalent conditions.
24	
25	We provide the instance `CategoryTheory.Category (TopCat.Sheaf C X)` as the full subcategory of
26	presheaves, and the fully faithful functor `Sheaf.forget : TopCat.Sheaf C X ⥤ TopCat.Presheaf C X`.
27	
28	-/
29	
30	@[expose] public section
31	
32	
33	universe w v u
34	
35	noncomputable section
36	
37	open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits TopologicalSpace Opposite TopologicalSpace.Opens
38	
39	namespace TopCat
40	
41	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C]
42	variable {X : TopCat.{w}} (F : Presheaf C X) {ι : Type v} (U : ι → Opens X)
43	
44	namespace Presheaf
45	
46	/-- The sheaf condition has several different equivalent formulations.
47	The official definition chosen here is in terms of Grothendieck topologies so that the results on
48	sites could be applied here easily, and this condition does not require additional constraints on
49	the value category.
50	The equivalent formulations of the sheaf condition on `presheaf C X` are as follows :
51	
52	1. `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheaf`: (the official definition)
53	  It is a sheaf with respect to the Grothendieck topology on `opens X`, which is to say:
54	  For each open cover `{ Uᵢ }` of `U`, and a family of compatible functions `A ⟶ F(Uᵢ)` for an
55	  `A : X`, there exists a unique gluing `A ⟶ F(U)` compatible with the restriction.
56	
57	2. `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafEqualizerProducts`: (requires `C` to have all products)
58	  For each open cover `{ Uᵢ }` of `U`, `F(U) ⟶ ∏ᶜ F(Uᵢ)` is the equalizer of the two morphisms
59	  `∏ᶜ F(Uᵢ) ⟶ ∏ᶜ F(Uᵢ ∩ Uⱼ)`.
60	  See `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts`.
61	
62	3. `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafOpensLeCover`:
63	  For each open cover `{ Uᵢ }` of `U`, `F(U)` is the limit of the diagram consisting of arrows
64	  `F(V₁) ⟶ F(V₂)` for every pair of open sets `V₁ ⊇ V₂` that are contained in some `Uᵢ`.
65	  See `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafOpensLeCover`.
66	
67	4. `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafPairwiseIntersections`:
68	  For each open cover `{ Uᵢ }` of `U`, `F(U)` is the limit of the diagram consisting of arrows
69	  from `F(Uᵢ)` and `F(Uⱼ)` to `F(Uᵢ ∩ Uⱼ)` for each pair `(i, j)`.
70	  See `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafPairwiseIntersections`.
71	
72	The following requires `C` to be concrete and complete, and `forget C` to reflect isomorphisms and
73	preserve limits. This applies to most "algebraic" categories, e.g. groups, abelian groups and rings.
74	
75	5. `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafUniqueGluing`:
76	  (requires `C` to be concrete and complete; `forget C` to reflect isomorphisms and preserve limits)
77	  For each open cover `{ Uᵢ }` of `U`, and a compatible family of elements `x : F(Uᵢ)`, there exists
78	  a unique gluing `x : F(U)` that restricts to the given elements.
79	  See `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing`.
80	
81	6. The underlying sheaf of types is a sheaf.
82	  See `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_comp` and
83	  `CategoryTheory.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget`.
84	-/
85	nonrec def IsSheaf (F : Presheaf.{w, v, u} C X) : Prop :=
86	  Presheaf.IsSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) F
87	
88	/-- The presheaf valued in `Unit` over any topological space is a sheaf.
89	-/
90	theorem isSheaf_unit (F : Presheaf (CategoryTheory.Discrete Unit) X) : F.IsSheaf :=
91	  fun x U S _ x _ => ⟨eqToHom (Subsingleton.elim _ _), by cat_disch, fun _ => by cat_disch⟩
92	
93	theorem isSheaf_iso_iff {F G : Presheaf C X} (α : F ≅ G) : F.IsSheaf ↔ G.IsSheaf :=
94	  Presheaf.isSheaf_of_iso_iff α
95	
96	/-- Transfer the sheaf condition across an isomorphism of presheaves.
97	-/
98	theorem isSheaf_of_iso {F G : Presheaf C X} (α : F ≅ G) (h : F.IsSheaf) : G.IsSheaf :=
99	  (isSheaf_iso_iff α).1 h
